EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front draped across northern NY and the VT-Canadian border on February 3rd slowly drifted south across much of NY and the VT/MA border by late in the day of February 4th. Light precipitation moved across northern NY during the morning and early afternoon hours of February 3rd with primarily snow in the St. Lawrence River valley and the Adirondacks' with light rain to light snow in the Champlain Valley.||However, during the late evening and overnight hours of February 3rd into the midday hours of February 4th, a series of weak low pressure areas moved northeast along the aforementioned boundary and brought steadier, heavier precipitation in the form of snow across northern NY, especially the Adirondacks and Champlain Valley. ||Storm total snowfall ranged from 6 to 10 inches across the St. Lawrence valley to 10 to 16 inches across the Adirondacks and the Champlain Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall ranged from 10 to 14 inches with much of this falling in the late evening of February 3rd to around daybreak on February 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to our north and west over the Great Lakes dragging a strong cold front through southeast NY. The front sparked severe thunderstorms that produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS site KFOK in Westhampton NY measured a 53 knot wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over southeastern New York that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NY Department of Highways reported several instances of downed trees with blocked/closed roads across Northern Westchester County between approximately 300 AM and 900 AM Local Standard Time. These instances included the towns of Crugers/Croton-On-Hudson, Cross River, South Salem, North Salem, Thornwood, Somers and Katonah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over southeastern New York that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NY Department of Highways reported multiple instances of downed trees blocking roadways in Southern Westchester County. A downed tree blocking two lanes was reported on northbound Hutchinson River Parkway south of exit 6B in Pelham, NY at 0359 AM Local Standard Time. Additionally, a tree down blocking 3 lanes in White Plains on northbound Sprain Brook Parkway north of Dobbs Ferry Road was reported at 409 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system passed to our north and west over the Great Lakes dragging a strong cold front through southeast NY. The front sparked severe thunderstorms that produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site XMOR in East Moriches NY measured a 53 knot wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moving through the area produced several rounds of heavy rainfall during the early morning of December 23rd. These rounds of heavy rainfall resulted in a widespread 1.5 to 3 inches of rainfall with localized areas of flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two lanes blocked on NY 306 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure well north of the area in Eastern Canada gave way to low pressure which moved up along and tracked very close to the coast.||Much of Orange County (geographically speaking) did not reach warning criteria as only far western portions of the county received warning criteria snowfall, while far eastern portions of the county received a little snow at the start, followed by mostly rain. A Winter Storm Watch was initially issued for Orange County, and subsequently a Winter Weather Advisory was issued. Most snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 4 inches, with 6 to 8 inch amounts reported in the vicinity of Port Jervis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 4.2 inches of snow in Pine Bush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over southeastern New York that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NY Department of Highways reported a tree down on Route 121 southbound at Red Fox Lane in Peach Lake, NY at 610 AM Local Standard Time. This blocked one lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave (mid-level disturbance) and a cold front moved into a marginal/moderate unstable airmass across northern NY during the afternoon of May 16th. Numerous showers and some scattered thunderstorms developed with a few storms producing severe weather in terms of localized damaging winds and/or hail of one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported with estimated hail size of a quarter (one-inch in diameter).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines downed by thunderstorm winds, as well as a few structures receiving minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level disturbance moved into a moderately unstable airmass across southern Ontario/southern Quebec and northern NY during the late afternoon/early evening of May 21st. Scattered thunderstorms in southern Ontario/Quebec intensified as they moved along the international border and began to develop southward. Isolated hail and strong winds were reported along the international border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic cold fronts and subsequent arctic high pressure and air masses moved into Ontario, Quebec on January 26th and 28th. These created dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 35 below zero in the Adirondacks and St. Lawrence valley with 20 to 29 below zero wind chills elsewhere. In addition, actual air temperatures on the morning of January 27th were 15 to 25 below zero and 10 to 25 below zero on the morning of the 29th with stronger winds on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold wind chills of 20 to 30 below zero late Wednesday night (26th) through mid-morning Thursday (27th) and again late Friday night (28th) into midday Saturday (29th). Actual air temperatures were 5 to 15 below zero as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough rotated through the lower Great Lakes with successive short wave troughs running through the amplified upper level flow. This brought a period of prolonged snows to the area with embedded heavier elements that were enhanced by lake effect and upslope snow processes. Snow accumulations were highly elevation dependent, and due to the orientation of the cold air over the area, this meant that areas in the higher terrain east of Lake Erie were the epicenter of heavy snowfall. Selected accumulations included 11.5 inches in Forestville, 11 inches in Cherry Creek, 13 inches in South Dayton, 10.3 inches in Colden, and 11.5 inches in East Concord.

An unphased low pressure system advanced from south to north through New England. Snow pushed northward to the west of it through all of New York State. Most all areas saw modest accumulations, however in the higher terrain of western New York, accumulations were heavier. The system snow was supplemented by light lake effect as the low pressure system advanced northward and colder air wrapped into the area. Because of the evolution of the system and the incoming cold air, heavy snow amounts were almost exclusively in the higher terrain east of Lake Erie. Selected snow totals included 7.5 inches in Hamburg, 8.3 inches in Mayville, 13 inches in Warsaw, and 10.4 inches in Franklinville.

Another arctic airmass moved into the region during Thursday night (1/20) and lingered through Saturday (1/22). Bitterly to dangerous cold wind chills of 20 to 35 below occurred overnight Thursday night - Friday morning. Actual air temperatures early Friday morning (1/21) were 10 to 35 below zero and 15 to 35 below zero on Saturday morning (1/22).||Record lows were set Friday morning at the only 3 climatological sites that records are used for such records. Massena was -30 (-27/2005), Saranac Lake was -31 (-27/2005) and Plattsburgh was -19 (-16/1984). Record lows for Saturday morning occurred at Saranac Lake with -31 (-30/2014) and Plattsburgh at -21 (-19/1984).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed low temperatures Friday and Saturday morning were 20 to 30 below zero.  Wind chill temperatures were about 10 degrees colder Thursday night into midday Friday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the region as a frontal system pushed through. A few of these storms became severe downing trees and power lines. A localized area of widespread straight-line wind damage occurred near Earlton in Greene County downing trees and wires as well as damaging structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced localized straight-line wind damage about 1 mile east of Earlton along New York Route 81. Wind descended down a hill south of Route 81 and moved north-northeastward across the road. Damage consisted of numerous snapped or uprooted trees and large branches, a broken metal windmill and the destruction of one tractor shed. Additionally, there was wind damage to the flashing of one house and two other homes were damaged by falling trees. Another house was damaged when a falling tree ripped electrical wires from it which pulled off some siding. Winds were estimated at 85 mph with a path length of one-quarter of a mile and path width of 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the region as a frontal system pushed through. A few of these storms became severe downing trees and power lines. A localized area of widespread straight-line wind damage occurred near Earlton in Greene County downing trees and wires as well as damaging structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto power lines on Sunset Road near East Greenbush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on August 17, 2022 in association with a disturbance off the Northeast US coast. These storms tracked from northeast to southwest. Damage was mainly confined to the Capital Region where a severe thunderstorm downed some trees and power lines across the region. In addition, sub-severe wind gusts led to some flying debris at the New York State Food Festival which was being held in Albany. Event managers cancelled the remainder of the event following the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across the region producing scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. One of the storms became severe leading to a downed tree in Saratoga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Grand Avenue resulting in a road closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred across portions of eastern New York ahead of an approaching cold front on October 13, 2022. Rainfall totals of 1 to 3 inches were common across the area. This rainfall led to one minor street flood report across Albany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small section of Route 85 in the Town of Bethlehem was closed between Mullens Road and Kenwood Avenue at the Albany County Rail Trail underpass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As high clouds from the remnants of Hurricane Ian cleared during the evening, winds lightened, and temperatures summarily plummeted. Frost was widespread across the area away from the urban centers, and freezing conditions occurred in the majority of Jefferson and Lewis counties. Selected low temperatures included 25 in Philadelphia, 28 in Watertown, 31 at Fort Drum, 27 in Harrisburg, and 30 in Lowville.

A strong cold front plowed across New York and triggered a narrow line of enhanced showers with little lightning. The line increased in strength over eastern Lake Ontario and continued across Jefferson and Lewis counties. A few notches were noted on the line on radar in a marginally favorable environment for weak tornadic spin-ups. While velocity values on radar were unimpressive for a tornado, and no tornado was observed, some wind damage did occur in Lewis County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Lowville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the region as a frontal system pushed through. A few of these storms became severe downing trees and power lines. A localized area of widespread straight-line wind damage occurred near Earlton in Greene County downing trees and wires as well as damaging structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto wires on Falls Road in the Town of Ghent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing the highest snowfall totals and strongest winds to eastern coastal sections. Blizzard conditions verified across Suffolk County on Long Island, with near blizzard conditions for Southern Queens and Nassau Counties. Islip, NY set a record for its highest calendar day (midnight to midnight) snowfall total with 23.5 inches. The storm total for Islip was 24.7 inches. Winds were a factor even further west into New York City and Southern Westchester were 6 inches or more of snow fell on average. East of New York City widespread amounts of 10 to 20 inches were reported across Long Island. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were reported from New York City on east into Long Island. A few peak wind gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported across Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near blizzard conditions occurred throughout Richmond County (Staten Island). Snowfall ranged from 6 to 10 inches with blowing and drifting. Broadcast media reported 10.1 inches in Huguenot. The public reported 6.8 inches in Todt Hill and 5.9 inches in Westerleigh.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and gusty winds impacted Southern Westchester. Snowfall reports from the public ranged mostly from 6 to 10 inches. Snowfall reports included; 9.8 inches was reported in New Rochelle, 9.5 in Rye, 9.4 in Mamaroneck, 9.2 in Port Chester, and 7.0 inches in Hastings-on-Hudson. Blowing and drifting of the snow occurred with Tappan Zee Light reporting a peak gust of 48 mph, and White Plains Airport (HPN) reporting a peak gust of 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the Carolinas rapidly intensified to 980 hPa as it tracked across eastern Pennsylvania and New York. This brought a deepening surface low track inland of the coast and the climatologically favored baroclinic zone along the periphery of the Gulf Stream. Other than the unusual track, it was a classic Nor���easter driven by a strong closed low across the southeast interacting with a longwave trough. Forcing for ascent was supported by strong differential vorticity advection ahead of the sharp mid-level closed low, impressive upper level coupled jet structure, and strongly diffluent flow aloft. A very strong southeasterly low level jet supported a strong warm conveyor belt, which resulted in a clearly defined deformation zone developing northwest of the storm early on January 17 and lingering over much of western New York with extreme snowfall rates for several hours.||Selected snowfall totals included 22 inches in Great Valley, 14 inches in Silver Creek, 15 inches on Boston, 20.6 inches at the Buffalo airport, 22 inches in Kenmore, 22.2 inches in Wyoming, 21 inches in Rushford, 17.8 inches in Bristol Harbor, 11 inches in Hamlin, 22 inches in Medina, 19.5 inches in Niagara Falls, 11.8 inches at the Rochester airport, 17 inches in Batavia, 12 inches in Dansville, 14.5 inches in Macedon, 12.1 inches in Redfield, 16 inches in Henderson, and 12.1 inches in Highmarket.

An arctic cold front moved across northern NY Friday afternoon (1/14) with a strong area of high pressure across south-central Canada building into NY by late Saturday into Sunday delivering sub-zero temperatures Friday night through Sunday morning. Simultaneously, a powerful ocean storm was moving into Newfoundland Canada Friday afternoon that creating brisk north-northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with higher gusts that combined with the arctic airmass created dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero overnight Friday night into Saturday morning.||Overnight air temperatures were 10 to 20 below zero Friday night-Saturday morning and 15 to 25 below zero Saturday night-Sunday morning.||These dangerously cold temperatures caused some postponements of outdoor activities, includng festivals and some ski resorts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero were observed across the region with actual air temperatures of 10 to 20 below zero Friday evening through midday Saturday. Overnight minimum temperatures Saturday night-Sunday morning were 15 to 25 below zero with calm/light winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region resulting in primarily freezing rain across eastern New York. Freezing rain began in the early morning hours and continued into the afternoon hours before tapering off. Some sleet and snowflakes mixed in across some areas but was rather brief. Overall flat ice accumulations of 0.10 to 0.30 of an inch were common. The icy conditions resulted in a few vehicle accidents and worship services and other facilities to be canceled or closed. The Ulster County Area Transit also canceled service due to the icy conditions.||Key impacts: transportation delays, vehicle accidents, business closures.

Snow moved into northern NY around midnight of January 17th with snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour at times during the morning, eventually ending by early afternoon with residual snow showers during the evening and night. ||The complexity of the storm due to its strength and strong low level jet accounted for mesoscale features of snowfall distribution with east-side upslope regions witnessing higher totals and west-side downwind communities witnessing less snowfall.||General snowfall accumulations in northern NY were 6 to 12 inches with some locally higher amounts in the St. Lawrence River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continued influx of Arctic air into the area resulted in yet another period of brutally cold wind chills in the North Country. The Great Lakes were continuing to cool from the warmer than average early winter, so a lack of ice cover resulted in attenuation of the cold air over most of western New York. This was not the case in Jefferson and Lewis counties, however, as wing chill factors dropped below -30 degrees Fahrenheit on multiple occasions. On January 21, this included Philadelphia at -46, Watertown at -32, Harrisburg at -38, and Copenhagen at -37. On January 24, this included Philadelphia at -44, Watertown at -37, and Copenhagen at -36.

Arctic high pressure moving from central Canada across the Great Lakes into the northeast on January 11th. Brisk northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph delivered sub-zero air temperatures, that combined created apparent temperatures (wind chill) in the 25 to 35 below zero range across the Adirondacks and along the Canadian border with  20 to 30 below zero elsewhere by daybreak on January 11th. ||High temperatures on the 11th ranged from single numbers above and below zero with wind chills still in the teens below zero. Low temperatures during the early night of the 11th were sub-zero and began to rise overnight due to a south wind. However, brisk south winds of 15 to 25 mph actually created wind chills in the 10 to 20 below zero range.||Several to numerous school districts closed school and after school activities due to the cold and COVID related complications according to local media.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures on the morning of the 11th (Tuesday) were in the 5 to 10 below zero with winds creating wind chills in the 15 to 25 below zero range. Daytime highs only reached the single numbers above zero with still sub-zero wind chills. Lows Tuesday night fell below zero early then rose overnight but combined with south winds of 10 to 20 mph still created wind chills in the 10 to 20 below zero range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved across northern NY Friday afternoon (1/14) with a strong area of high pressure across south-central Canada building into NY by late Saturday into Sunday delivering sub-zero temperatures Friday night through Sunday morning. Simultaneously, a powerful ocean storm was moving into Newfoundland Canada Friday afternoon that creating brisk north-northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with higher gusts that combined with the arctic airmass created dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero overnight Friday night into Saturday morning.||Overnight air temperatures were 10 to 20 below zero Friday night-Saturday morning and 15 to 25 below zero Saturday night-Sunday morning.||These dangerously cold temperatures caused some postponements of outdoor activities, includng festivals and some ski resorts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero were observed across the region with actual air temperatures of 10 to 20 below zero Friday evening through midday Saturday. Overnight minimum temperatures Saturday night-Sunday morning were 15 to 25 below zero with calm/light winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long tracked and powerful winter storm, with a history from the Mississippi River valley through the southeast and north along the Appalachians into the northeast, impacted NY on January 17th. Snow moved into northern NY around midnight of January 17th with snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour at times during the morning, eventually ending by early afternoon with residual snow showers during the evening and night. ||The complexity of the storm due to its strength and strong low level jet accounted for mesoscale features of snowfall distribution with east-side upslope regions witnessing higher totals and west-side downwind communities witnessing less snowfall.||General snowfall accumulations in northern NY were 6 to 12 inches with some locally higher amounts in the St. Lawrence River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front associated with a surface low pressure system passed through Central New York, kicking off an initial round of a snow. The low then passed through the region, bringing additional snowfall. Snowfall reports generally ranged between 6 and 14 inches. Winds were gusty as this sytem passed through the region and resulted in blowing and drifting snow.

A high pressure was present over Ontario/Quebec with a tight pressure gradient extending across Central New York. Subzero temperatures and strong, gusty winds brought wind chills as low as 35 below zero in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values of -25��F to -29��F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving area of low pressure dragged a cold front across the region. Windy conditions occurred behind the front with gusts in the 45-55 mph range. Cold air advection occurred behind the front, however it was slow and took awhile for lake-induced instability to get lake snows going. Further, a pronounced dry slot evolved such that the sun even came out. This slow cold advection and chunk of dry air took the bulk of Wednesday night to overcome. A southwest flow continued across the region with lake snows east of Lake Erie eventually organizing before sunrise Thursday. There were several hours of snowfall rates of 3 inches per hour at the Buffalo airport. The Skyway in downtown Buffalo was closed late morning and into the afternoon for poor visibility in snow. A lightning strike was recorded just to the west of Crystal Beach around noontime. Around 1-2 PM, the snow band lifted northward across the Northtowns and weakened some as it became slightly detached from the lake. Around 3-4 PM, the snow band started to settle back southward.||Off Lake Ontario, the lake effect snow band organized well around 9 AM. This band lifted northward from the Tug Hill to Watertown shortly after noontime. Snowfall rates were measured by a spotter of 3 inches per hour between 3:30 pm and 4:30 pm. The band of snow then dropped back and focused upon the Tug Hill by late afternoon, where it remained nearly stationary into the early evening hours.||Selected storm total snowfall amounts included 16.6 inches in Depew, 18.2 inches at the Buffalo airport, 19.8 inches in Watertown, 7 inches in Corfu, 16.5 inches in Cassadaga, 11 inches in Copenhagen, 13 inches in Fulton, 9 inches in Cato, and 14 inches in Little Valley.

The continued influx of Arctic air into the area resulted in yet another period of brutally cold wind chills in the North Country. The Great Lakes were continuing to cool from the warmer than average early winter, so a lack of ice cover resulted in attenuation of the cold air over most of western New York. This was not the case in Jefferson and Lewis counties, however, as wing chill factors dropped below -30 degrees Fahrenheit on multiple occasions. On January 26, this included Watertown at -36, Cape Vincent at -31, and Copenhagen at -33. On January 27, this included Philadelphia at -36 and Watertown at -32. On January 30, this included Watertown at -39, Belleville at -35, and Copenhagen at -35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing the highest snowfall totals and strongest winds to eastern coastal sections. Blizzard conditions verified across Suffolk County on Long Island, with near blizzard conditions for Southern Queens and Nassau Counties. Islip, NY set a record for its highest calendar day (midnight to midnight) snowfall total with 23.5 inches. The storm total for Islip was 24.7 inches. Winds were a factor even further west into New York City and Southern Westchester were 6 inches or more of snow fell on average. East of New York City widespread amounts of 10 to 20 inches were reported across Long Island. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were reported from New York City on east into Long Island. A few peak wind gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported across Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Islip, NY set a record for its highest calendar day (midnight to midnight) snowfall total with 23.5 inches. The storm total for Islip was 24.7 inches. Blizzard conditions began approximately at 7 am and continued through and past 10 am with visibilities at and below 1/4 mile with consistent wind gusts exceeding 35 mph. CWOP, ASOS, and mesonet data recorded numerous wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph, with Islip (ISP) recording a 51 mph gust, and Shirley (HWV) recording a 52 mph gust. Numerous reports of over 20 inches of snow which included reports from NWS Employees and Official NWS Observations.

Lows Tuesday night fell below zero early then rose overnight but combined with south winds of 10 to 20 mph still created wind chills in the 15 to 20 below zero range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic airmass moved into the region during Thursday night (1/20) and lingered through Saturday (1/22). Bitterly to dangerous cold wind chills of 20 to 35 below occurred overnight Thursday night - Friday morning. Actual air temperatures early Friday morning (1/21) were 10 to 35 below zero and 15 to 35 below zero on Saturday morning (1/22).||Record lows were set Friday morning at the only 3 climatological sites that records are used for such records. Massena was -30 (-27/2005), Saranac Lake was -31 (-27/2005) and Plattsburgh was -19 (-16/1984). Record lows for Saturday morning occurred at Saranac Lake with -31 (-30/2014) and Plattsburgh at -21 (-19/1984).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed low temperatures Friday and Saturday morning were 20 to 30 below zero.  Wind chill temperatures were about 10 degrees colder Thursday night into midday Friday. |Massena set a daily record on Friday at -30 degrees (-27/2005).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing the highest snowfall totals and strongest winds to eastern coastal sections. Blizzard conditions verified across Suffolk County on Long Island, with near blizzard conditions for Southern Queens and Nassau Counties. Islip, NY set a record for its highest calendar day (midnight to midnight) snowfall total with 23.5 inches. The storm total for Islip was 24.7 inches. Winds were a factor even further west into New York City and Southern Westchester were 6 inches or more of snow fell on average. East of New York City widespread amounts of 10 to 20 inches were reported across Long Island. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were reported from New York City on east into Long Island. A few peak wind gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported across Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observers, trained spotters, NWS employee, mesonet stations, and radar confirmed a blizzard across Northeastern Suffolk County. Numerous reports of drifts to and above 3 feet along with multiple snowfall reports above 20 inches, along with wind gusts reported from CWOP and mesonet stations confirmed consistent wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph. The CWOP station in Baiting Hollow reported a 71 mph wind gust, and sorrounding CWOP and mesonet stations like Napeague and Stony Brook reported wind gusts over 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near blizzard conditions across Northern Nassau with drifts 2 to 3 feet in spots, with peak wind gusts reported of 48 mph at NYSM in Wantagh, and 40 mph at Muttontown CWOP. Snowfall totals on averaged ranged from 8 to 12 inches across Western Northern Nassau, and 12 to 18 inches across the eastern half of Northern Nassau. The general public reporting 19.2 inches in Levittown and 17.2 inches in Plainedge. An NWS Employee reported 11.5 inches in Hicksville. The general public reported 10.4 inches in Manhasset Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system approached from the southwest at the surface along with a closed upper level low. A broken like of convection produced strong to severe wind gusts across southern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station BRON measured a 50 KT wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving area of low pressure dragged a cold front across the region. Windy conditions occurred behind the front with gusts in the 45-55 mph range. Cold air advection occurred behind the front, however it was slow and took awhile for lake-induced instability to get lake snows going. Further, a pronounced dry slot evolved such that the sun even came out. This slow cold advection and chunk of dry air took the bulk of Wednesday night to overcome. A southwest flow continued across the region with lake snows east of Lake Erie eventually organizing before sunrise Thursday. There were several hours of snowfall rates of 3 inches per hour at the Buffalo airport. The Skyway in downtown Buffalo was closed late morning and into the afternoon for poor visibility in snow. A lightning strike was recorded just to the west of Crystal Beach around noontime. Around 1-2 PM, the snow band lifted northward across the Northtowns and weakened some as it became slightly detached from the lake. Around 3-4 PM, the snow band started to settle back southward.||Off Lake Ontario, the lake effect snow band organized well around 9 AM. This band lifted northward from the Tug Hill to Watertown shortly after noontime. Snowfall rates were measured by a spotter of 3 inches per hour between 3:30 pm and 4:30 pm. The band of snow then dropped back and focused upon the Tug Hill by late afternoon, where it remained nearly stationary into the early evening hours.||Selected storm total snowfall amounts included 16.6 inches in Depew, 18.2 inches at the Buffalo airport, 19.8 inches in Watertown, 7 inches in Corfu, 16.5 inches in Cassadaga, 11 inches in Copenhagen, 13 inches in Fulton, 9 inches in Cato, and 14 inches in Little Valley.

Arctic high pressure moving from central Canada across the Great Lakes into the northeast on January 11th. Brisk northwest winds of 10 to 20 mph delivered sub-zero air temperatures, that combined created apparent temperatures (wind chill) in the 25 to 35 below zero range across the Adirondacks and along the Canadian border with  20 to 30 below zero elsewhere by daybreak on January 11th. ||High temperatures on the 11th ranged from single numbers above and below zero with wind chills still in the teens below zero. Low temperatures during the early night of the 11th were sub-zero and began to rise overnight due to a south wind. However, brisk south winds of 15 to 25 mph actually created wind chills in the 10 to 20 below zero range.||Several to numerous school districts closed school and after school activities due to the cold and COVID related complications according to local media.

Daytime highs only reached near zero with still sub-zero wind chills. Lows Tuesday night fell below zero early then rose overnight but combined with south winds of 10 to 20 mph still created wind chills in the 15 to 20 below zero range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing the highest snowfall totals and strongest winds to eastern coastal sections. Blizzard conditions verified across Suffolk County on Long Island, with near blizzard conditions for Southern Queens and Nassau Counties. Islip, NY set a record for its highest calendar day (midnight to midnight) snowfall total with 23.5 inches. The storm total for Islip was 24.7 inches. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches across Orange County, with a couple of more local higher amounts. A larger majority of the county did not reach warning criteria, with a couple of smaller pockets reaching over 6 inches. A trained spotter in Highland Mills reported 8.4 inches and 5.8 inches in Warwick, and there was a public report of 6.9 inches in Greenwood Lake. These higher totals were reported in the SW part of the county. The remainder of the county had reports under 6 inches, and mainly closer to 3 and 4 inches. A trained spotter reported 5.0 inches in Blooming Grove. Trained spotters also reported the following; 4.0 in Gardnertown, 3.5 in Middletown, 3.5 in Otisville, and 2.5 in Pine Bush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the Carolinas rapidly intensified to 980 hPa as it tracked across eastern Pennsylvania and New York. This brought a deepening surface low track inland of the coast and the climatologically favored baroclinic zone along the periphery of the Gulf Stream. Other than the unusual track, it was a classic Nor���easter driven by a strong closed low across the southeast interacting with a longwave trough. Forcing for ascent was supported by strong differential vorticity advection ahead of the sharp mid-level closed low, impressive upper level coupled jet structure, and strongly diffluent flow aloft. A very strong southeasterly low level jet supported a strong warm conveyor belt, which resulted in a clearly defined deformation zone developing northwest of the storm early on January 17 and lingering over much of western New York with extreme snowfall rates for several hours.||Selected snowfall totals included 22 inches in Great Valley, 14 inches in Silver Creek, 15 inches on Boston, 20.6 inches at the Buffalo airport, 22 inches in Kenmore, 22.2 inches in Wyoming, 21 inches in Rushford, 17.8 inches in Bristol Harbor, 11 inches in Hamlin, 22 inches in Medina, 19.5 inches in Niagara Falls, 11.8 inches at the Rochester airport, 17 inches in Batavia, 12 inches in Dansville, 14.5 inches in Macedon, 12.1 inches in Redfield, 16 inches in Henderson, and 12.1 inches in Highmarket.

A mid-level trough and cold front crossed the eastern Great Lakes region through the evening hours of Saturday, January 22. Bands of lake effect snow formed quickly after the cold front passage, but off Lake Erie, the snow band was initially weak and at times disorganized. Still gusty winds behind the cold front and the mixing in of drier air held back on the formation of the Lake Erie band of snow until a period of time after the cold front.||Once moisture deepened later in the night and winds in the lower 5 thousand feet of |the atmosphere diminished from around 45 knots down to 25 to 30 knots, plumes of |lake effect snow off the lakes intensified further. ||The Lake Erie snow band started across the Buffalo Southtowns during the late |evening hours. Initially the band was broad, intensifying briefly just past |midnight. As deeper moisture arrived in the later overnight period a more |concentrated band of snow oriented across far southern Erie County northeastward|into Wyoming County. The snow band peaked in intensity, with snowfall rates |2 to 3/hr, just past dawn before weakening through the morning and afternoon |hours across Ski Country. ||The Lake Ontario snow band started just to the north of Watertown late Saturday |evening. Along a much longer fetch of lake waters that contributed additional |moisture as well as convergence this band of snow became intense quickly, dropping|snow across northern Jefferson through the midnight hour. As winds aloft veered |the snow band oriented across the northern Tug Hill just past midnight, and |intensified with snowfall rates likely 2 to 3 inches per hour across southern Jefferson |County. This band of snow remained over southern Jefferson and into Lewis County|till the dawn hours producing up to a foot and a half snowfall. The snow band |continued its southward movement, dropping to the southeast Lake Ontario |shoreline through the midday hours in a weakening state.||Another clipper low advanced across the eastern Great Lakes Sunday afternoon, |January 23. The increased wind shear around the low disrupted the snow bands, |with diffuse snow bands within widespread light synoptic snow through the afternoon |hours.||Selected snow totals from this event included 17 inches at Lake Erie Beach, 13 inches in Angola, 14 inches in Henderson, and 11.2 inches in Watertown.

A intense low pressure system tracks just inland from the coast as high pressure retreated resulting in wintry precipitation changing quickly to rain along the coast and in the city. The changeover took longer for northern and northwestern sections where accumulating snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports came in with 5.0 inches in Buchanan, 4.5 inches in Peekskill, 4.5 inches in Golden's Bridge, and 2.0 inches in Croton on the Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A intense low pressure system tracks just inland from the coast as high pressure retreated resulting in wintry precipitation changing quickly to rain along the coast and in the city. The changeover took longer for northern and northwestern sections where accumulating snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated, and then changed to freezing rain, with the freezing rain lasting for several hours across far western portions of Orange County. Port Jervis Cocorahs reported 8.4 inches of snow, with Middletown Fire Department reporting 6.2 inches, a trained spotter in Monroe reporting 5.8 inches, and a public report from Gardnertown reporting 4.2 inches. Montgomery ASOS reported .14 inches of ice from freezing rain. The vast majority of Orange County fell below warning criteria, with only far western portions reporting warning criteria snow, snow / ice.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: From surrounding reports from trained spotters and official NWS Observations along with radar indicated an estimated 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across Putnam County before a brief changeover to freezing rain, then to rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing the highest snowfall totals and strongest winds to eastern coastal sections. Blizzard conditions verified across Suffolk County on Long Island, with near blizzard conditions for Southern Queens and Nassau Counties. Islip, NY set a record for its highest calendar day (midnight to midnight) snowfall total with 23.5 inches. The storm total for Islip was 24.7 inches. Winds were a factor even further west into New York City and Southern Westchester were 6 inches or more of snow fell on average. East of New York City widespread amounts of 10 to 20 inches were reported across Long Island. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were reported from New York City on east into Long Island. A few peak wind gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported across Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Islip, NY set a record for its highest calendar day (midnight to midnight) snowfall total with 23.5 inches. The storm total for Islip was 24.7 inches. Numerous reports of over 20 inches of snow which included reports from NWS Employees and Official NWS Observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving area of low pressure dragged a cold front across the region. Windy conditions occurred behind the front with gusts in the 45-55 mph range. Cold air advection occurred behind the front, however it was slow and took awhile for lake-induced instability to get lake snows going. Further, a pronounced dry slot evolved such that the sun even came out. This slow cold advection and chunk of dry air took the bulk of Wednesday night to overcome. A southwest flow continued across the region with lake snows east of Lake Erie eventually organizing before sunrise Thursday. There were several hours of snowfall rates of 3 inches per hour at the Buffalo airport. The Skyway in downtown Buffalo was closed late morning and into the afternoon for poor visibility in snow. A lightning strike was recorded just to the west of Crystal Beach around noontime. Around 1-2 PM, the snow band lifted northward across the Northtowns and weakened some as it became slightly detached from the lake. Around 3-4 PM, the snow band started to settle back southward.||Off Lake Ontario, the lake effect snow band organized well around 9 AM. This band lifted northward from the Tug Hill to Watertown shortly after noontime. Snowfall rates were measured by a spotter of 3 inches per hour between 3:30 pm and 4:30 pm. The band of snow then dropped back and focused upon the Tug Hill by late afternoon, where it remained nearly stationary into the early evening hours.||Selected storm total snowfall amounts included 16.6 inches in Depew, 18.2 inches at the Buffalo airport, 19.8 inches in Watertown, 7 inches in Corfu, 16.5 inches in Cassadaga, 11 inches in Copenhagen, 13 inches in Fulton, 9 inches in Cato, and 14 inches in Little Valley.

A middle and upper level trough dug across the eastern Great Lakes with 850 hPa temperatures dropping to -21 degrees Celsius at Buffalo early on January 9. The pattern aloft was highly favorable for significant snows east of Lake Ontario. Moisture was supplied by upstream lake connections along with the long fetch across Lake Ontario in a westerly flow. This supported the development of a singular band of heavy snow. The end result were highly impressive one day storm total snowfalls of around 30 inches in Osceola, 23 inches in Redfield, and 8 inches in Lacona.

An arctic cold front moved across northern NY Friday afternoon (1/14) with a strong area of high pressure across south-central Canada building into NY by late Saturday into Sunday delivering sub-zero temperatures Friday night through Sunday morning. Simultaneously, a powerful ocean storm was moving into Newfoundland Canada Friday afternoon that creating brisk north-northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with higher gusts that combined with the arctic airmass created dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 40 below zero overnight Friday night into Saturday morning.||Overnight air temperatures were 10 to 20 below zero Friday night-Saturday morning and 15 to 25 below zero Saturday night-Sunday morning.||These dangerously cold temperatures caused some postponements of outdoor activities, includng festivals and some ski resorts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills of 25 to 35 below zero were observed across the region with actual air temperatures of 10 to 20 below zero Friday evening through midday Saturday. Overnight minimum temperatures Saturday night-Sunday morning were 10 to 20 below zero with calm/light winds.

Low pressure across the Carolinas rapidly intensified to 980 hPa as it tracked across eastern Pennsylvania and New York. This brought a deepening surface low track inland of the coast and the climatologically favored baroclinic zone along the periphery of the Gulf Stream. Other than the unusual track, it was a classic Nor���easter driven by a strong closed low across the southeast interacting with a longwave trough. Forcing for ascent was supported by strong differential vorticity advection ahead of the sharp mid-level closed low, impressive upper level coupled jet structure, and strongly diffluent flow aloft. A very strong southeasterly low level jet supported a strong warm conveyor belt, which resulted in a clearly defined deformation zone developing northwest of the storm early on January 17 and lingering over much of western New York with extreme snowfall rates for several hours.||Selected snowfall totals included 22 inches in Great Valley, 14 inches in Silver Creek, 15 inches on Boston, 20.6 inches at the Buffalo airport, 22 inches in Kenmore, 22.2 inches in Wyoming, 21 inches in Rushford, 17.8 inches in Bristol Harbor, 11 inches in Hamlin, 22 inches in Medina, 19.5 inches in Niagara Falls, 11.8 inches at the Rochester airport, 17 inches in Batavia, 12 inches in Dansville, 14.5 inches in Macedon, 12.1 inches in Redfield, 16 inches in Henderson, and 12.1 inches in Highmarket.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region resulting in primarily freezing rain across eastern New York. Freezing rain began in the early morning hours and continued into the afternoon hours before tapering off. Some sleet and snowflakes mixed in across some areas but was rather brief. Overall flat ice accumulations of 0.10 to 0.30 of an inch were common. The icy conditions resulted in a few vehicle accidents and worship services and other facilities to be canceled or closed. The Ulster County Area Transit also canceled service due to the icy conditions.||Key impacts: transportation delays, vehicle accidents, business closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region resulting in primarily freezing rain across eastern New York. Freezing rain began in the early morning hours and continued into the afternoon hours before tapering off. Some sleet and snowflakes mixed in across some areas but was rather brief. Overall flat ice accumulations of 0.10 to 0.30 of an inch were common. The icy conditions resulted in a few vehicle accidents and worship services and other facilities to be canceled or closed. The Ulster County Area Transit also canceled service due to the icy conditions.||Key impacts: transportation delays, vehicle accidents, business closures.

A Nor'easter tracked just east of the benchmark bringing the highest snowfall totals and strongest winds to eastern coastal sections. Blizzard conditions verified across Suffolk County on Long Island, with near blizzard conditions for Southern Queens and Nassau Counties. Islip, NY set a record for its highest calendar day (midnight to midnight) snowfall total with 23.5 inches. The storm total for Islip was 24.7 inches. Winds were a factor even further west into New York City and Southern Westchester were 6 inches or more of snow fell on average. East of New York City widespread amounts of 10 to 20 inches were reported across Long Island. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were reported from New York City on east into Long Island. A few peak wind gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported across Suffolk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near blizzard conditions occurred across Southern Queens. The JFK ASOS reported numerous 1/4 mile visibility at the height of the storm, along with a peak wind gust of 46 mph. Snowfall reports were as follows: JFK Official NWS Observaion of 11.0 inches, a trained spotter in Howard Beach reported 11.5 inches, a trained spotter in Jackson Heights reported 11.3 inches, and a public report 2 miles south of Elmhurst reported 12.7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced strong winds over Southeastern New York early in the morning of February 18, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located 1 mile west of East Northport, NY at 355 AM Local Standard Time. Additionally, a NWS Employee reported a large tree down in Centereach, NY at 720 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced strong winds over Southeastern New York early in the morning of February 18, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of high wind gusts were recorded at mesonet stations - both located in Orient, NY. One measured gust of 68 mph occurred at 400 AM Local Standard Time. The other gust was measured at 61 mph and occurred at 409 AM Local Standard time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west. This produced strong winds over Southeastern New York early in the morning of February 18, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high wind gust of 66 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in East Moriches, NY at 435 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west while a low pressure center passed through New England. This produced strong winds over Southeastern New York during the late afternoon to early evening of February 19, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts occurred including a measured wind gust of 62 mph at 628 PM Local Standard Time at a mesonet in Stony Brook (measurement taken at 159 feet above ground), and a measured wind gust of 64 mph at 801 PM Local Standard Time at a mesonet in Eatons Neck (elevation of 71 feet above ground).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient tightened as a cold front approached from the west while a low pressure center passed through New England. This produced strong winds over Southeastern New York during the late afternoon to early evening of February 19, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts were measured by mesonet stations. A gust of 61 mph was measured at an elevation of 78 feet above ground level in Baiting Hollow, NY at 420 PM Local Standard Time. A gust of 61 mph was also measured at an elevation of 53 feet above ground level at Great Gull Island, NY at 456 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure off the New England coast combined with low pressure across the Great Lakes created a strong south to north pressure gradient across NY/VT. The south-north channel of the Champlain Valley helped funnel these strong winds in the climatological favored areas of northern VT portions of the Champlain Valley.||South winds of 20 to 35 mph with frequent gusts in excess of 45 mph occurred across much of the Champlain Valley of NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph were common across Clinton county with the higher gusts along and near the lakeshore. Twigs, small branches downed by winds were the main impacts as well as delays caused to the Cumberland Head-Grand Isle ferry across Lake Champlain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the area brought multiple rounds of rain on April 7th. The front approached and a low pressure system developed over the area resulting in moderate to heavy rain over the Lower Hudson Valley in New York. 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over a several hour period. This resulted in river and poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Car submerged in flood water on South Pascack Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of an arctic cold front, arrival of arctic high pressure, gusty winds and very cold air led to wind chill values between -15 and -30 degrees Fahrenheit across portions of eastern New York during the overnight and early morning hours on Tuesday, January 11. In preparation for the arrival of cold air, a Code Blue was put in effect for the Capital Region which allowed for emergency overnight shelters to be open for anyone needing assistance.

Lake effect snow developed on the night of the 9th and lasted through the 10th. The heaviest snowfall totals were 14 to 23 inches in the main portion of the band that was present over Northern Oneida County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 14 and 23 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian high was over Central New York the night of January 26th. Clear skies allowed temperatures to quickly fall well below zero. Winds were generally calm, so wind chill was not a concern across most of the region. These conditions did result in a fatality in Otsego County after a college student had been exposed to the sub-zero temperatures for an extend period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A college student walked onto the grounds of a bus garage at around 2:15am but was not found until 7am. He had suffered from long-term exposure to sub-zero temperatures and was later pronounced dead at the hospital. Temperatures early in the morning ranged from -8 to -12��F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms developed along a cold front that swept through Central New York. While winds were gusty, most gusts were below severe criteria. Some stronger gusts were observed in Broome County where power lines and a transformer were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down wires. As a result, a transformer caught on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front slowly moved from northwest to southeast across northern NY during the late evening of February 17th and overnight hours of February 18th, bringing much colder air. Meanwhile, a weak surface wave was traveling northeast along this boundary. The combination allowed for light rain to change to freezing rain and eventually sleet and snow through the morning hours of February 18th.||Ice accumulations were generally around a tenth (0.10) of an inch with sleet/snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches that created hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to freezing rain and accumulated to a tenth of an inch or less, followed by 1/2 to 1 inch of sleet then snowfall accumulating 1-2 inches created hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region on Saturday, February 19, 2022. A passing warm front brought a period of light snow to the region during the morning and early afternoon hours with mainly a coating to two inches of accumulation. This snow was enough to cause a few vehicle spinouts/accidents, especially along interstates 87 and 90.||In the wake of this light snow, snow showers and squalls developed ahead of a cold front and pushed eastward during the afternoon hours, briefly but rapidly reducing visibility to at or less than one quarter of a mile at times with occasional wind gusts between 25 and 40 mph. A quick additional coating of snow occurred from these snow showers.||In the wake of the squalls and cold front, strong wind gusts on the order of 35 to around 50 mph developed, continuing into the evening hours before easing at or just before midnight.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents/spinouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Area webcams showed visibilities estimated to be reduced to around one quarter of a mile. Wind gusts up to 25 mph were observed.

A storm system pushed across the region during February 17-18, 2022. Warm air pushed northward into the region along with periods of rainfall before a cold front brought the return of windy and colder weather. A convective line of gusty showers also accompanied the cold frontal passage. Some locations picked up over 1 inch of rainfall which led to minor flooding in a few locations. ||In the wake of the cold front, strong winds affected areas mainly along and south of I-90 with gusts 40 to 55 mph observed, resulted in a few downed trees. Some power outages occurred as a result of the wind, mainly across Ulster County.||The warm and rainy weather ahead of the cold front resulted in some snow melt as well as some ice to break up on area rivers and streams. As the colder weather returned, localized ice jams formed, some of which led to flooding, including in Dolgeville and Canajoharie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The westbound lane of State Highway 5 (West Main Street) between Water Street and Budnick Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure developed along a frontal wave along the southeast coastline and moved northeast offshore as 700 mb frontogenesis contributed to sub advisory and advisory level snowfall across Southeastern New York. Impacts were minimal as the snow was wet in nature and had difficulty sticking on cement surfaces. Not all zones are entered as with a warm ground some zones had under 2.5 inches of accumulated snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3.5 to 5.8 inches fell across a large swath of NW Suffolk County with reports including NWS Employees, Trained Spotters, and the public. Trained spotters reported 5.8 inches in both Smithtown and East Setauket at the end of the event.

Impacts were minimal as the snow was wet in nature and had difficulty sticking on cement surfaces. Not all zones are entered as with a warm ground some zones had under 2.5 inches of accumulated snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports were generally at 3 inches with a trained spotter reporting 3.0 inches in Golden's Bridge, and the public reporting 3.0 inches in North Castle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure developed along a frontal wave along the southeast coastline and moved northeast offshore as 700 mb frontogenesis contributed to sub advisory and advisory level snowfall across Southeastern New York. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the public reported snowfall amounts of 2.8 to 4.5 inches. The general public reported 4.5 inches at 2 SW Greenwood Lake, and a trained spotter reported 4.0 inches in Gardnertown. A public report came in with 3.9 inches at 2 SSW of Stewart Airport. All these reports came in at the end of accumulating snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which behaved like an anafront as the upper level flow was parallel to the front brought advisory freezing rain amounts to portions of the area. The highest freezing rain totals were over northwestern areas like Orange County where a few reports of over a quarter inch of ice, and near a half inch of ice took place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 0.35 inches of flat ice from 2 E Washingtonville, and 0.13 inches of radial ice from 3 SSW Pine Bush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast along the Tennessee Valley and the Lower Appalachains and redeveloped along the Mid Atlantic coast as it moved northeast. A brief mix of sleet and snow changed to freezing rain, and eventually plain rain before ending. Snow amounts were fairly negligible except in far northern and northwestern areas where approximately a couple of inches of snow and sleet fell before the change to freezing rain and even plain rain in most places. No impact LSRs were issued / reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing Rain of 0.15 inches was reported by the Shirley, Brookhaven ASOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Monroe reported 0.21 inches of ice accretion and 2.3 inches combined of snow and sleet. The Montgomery ASOS only reported 0.03 inches of ice accretion. A cooperative observer reported 3.0 inches of snow at 0.8 N Port Jervis. A general 1 to 3 inches of a combination of snow and in most places some sleet were reported across Orange County, mainly before a changeover to freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region during the morning and early afternoon hours on Friday, February 25, 2022 producing a period of snow, heavy at times, with sleet and freezing rain mixing in at times for portions of the eastern Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from as low as 2 to 4 inches (common in parts of the mid-Hudson Valley) to as much as 6 to 9 inches elsewhere. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the morning rush hour with snowfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour at that time, resulting in significant impacts including several vehicle accidents with some minor injuries reported.||The New York State Thruway reduced its speed limit to 45 mph during the early morning hours due to the difficult travel conditions. Many area colleges and universities cancelled or delayed classes. Dozens of outgoing and incoming flights were cancelled at the Albany International Airport. ||The snowstorm led to the issuance of snow emergencies for some municipalities including Kingston, Catskill, Athens, Greenport, Cairo and Chatham.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, flight cancellations, school closures.

Showers and thunderstorms formed along a sea breeze boundary across Long Island. One of the thunderstorms was able to become severe and caused damaging winds as well as large hail in Central Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches and several tree tops snapped off in the area of Sachem North High School and Smith Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed along a sea breeze boundary across Long Island. One of the thunderstorms was able to become severe and caused damaging winds as well as large hail in Central Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on powerlines near Portion Road and College Road in Farmingville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed along a sea breeze boundary across Long Island. One of the thunderstorms was able to become severe and caused damaging winds as well as large hail in Central Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures of large tree limbs down on road. Power was lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure developed along a frontal wave along the southeast coastline and moved northeast offshore as 700 mb frontogenesis contributed to sub advisory and advisory level snowfall across Southeastern New York. EVENT_NARRATIVE: LaGuardia Airport measured 2.4 inches of snow and a trained spotter measured 3.1 inches in Little Neck.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports were generally at 3 inches. The public reported 3.3 inches in Bronxville and 3.2 inches in Larchmont. A trained spotter reported 3.0 inches in White Plains. A public report came in with 4.3 inches at Hastings-on-the-Hudson when the event was finished.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 2.8 inches at 1 E Harlem. There were reports of 3 inches right along and just north of the Westchester Bronx border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the region bringing mild and windy weather along with periods of rainfall, some heavy enough, to cause localized areas of flooding. An ice jam also developed in the Village of Dolgeville leading to flooding along the East Canada Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain and snowmelt across the region led to ice jam flooding along the East Canada Creek along Dolge Avenue and Van Buren Street in the Village of Dolgeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system pushed across the region during February 17-18, 2022. Warm air pushed northward into the region along with periods of rainfall before a cold front brought the return of windy and colder weather. A convective line of gusty showers also accompanied the cold frontal passage. Some locations picked up over 1 inch of rainfall which led to minor flooding in a few locations. ||In the wake of the cold front, strong winds affected areas mainly along and south of I-90 with gusts 40 to 55 mph observed, resulted in a few downed trees. Some power outages occurred as a result of the wind, mainly across Ulster County.||The warm and rainy weather ahead of the cold front resulted in some snow melt as well as some ice to break up on area rivers and streams. As the colder weather returned, localized ice jams formed, some of which led to flooding, including in Dolgeville and Canajoharie.

A frontal boundary slowly sagged southward through the area. This allowed for deep cold air to make its way south of the Pennsylvania state line. A series of weak disturbances then worked down this front bringing several rounds of moderate to heavy snow. The heaviest snow fell in the evening of February 3 for most areas. This occurred after a slow changeover from rain to snow as the front sagged southward. By the end of the event, many portions of the area received more than a foot of snow. Selected reports include 16.5 inches in Lockport, 12.5 inches in Kenmore, 12.3 inches in Gerry, 16.8 inches in Perrysburg, 13.6 inches in Sardinia, 10.2 inches in Medina, 15 inches in Webster, 12 inches in Batavia, 13 inches in Attica, 12.6 inches in Dansville, 10.8 inches in Alfred, 14 inches in Marion, 11.5 inches in Fulton, 8.3 inches in Watertown, 11.5 inches in Constableville, and 9.2 inches in Bristol Harbor.

A cold front which behaved like an anafront as the upper level flow was parallel to the front brought advisory freezing rain amounts to portions of the area. The highest freezing rain totals were over northwestern areas like Orange County where a few reports of over a quarter inch of ice, and near a half inch of ice took place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 0.71 of flat ice accretion in Monroe. This was treated as an isolated occurrence as not enough of zone had reports close enough to 0.50 to constitute as an ice storm entry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Ohio River Valley across southern New England on February 25th. Snow developed across NY in the early morning hours and ended by mid-afternoon with snowfall amounts of 5 to 10 inches across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 10 inches were common, the heaviest occurring in the latter third of the event.

An area of low pressure pushed across the region on Saturday, February 19, 2022. A passing warm front brought a period of light snow to the region during the morning and early afternoon hours with mainly a coating to two inches of accumulation. This snow was enough to cause a few vehicle spinouts/accidents, especially along interstates 87 and 90.||In the wake of this light snow, snow showers and squalls developed ahead of a cold front and pushed eastward during the afternoon hours, briefly but rapidly reducing visibility to at or less than one quarter of a mile at times with occasional wind gusts between 25 and 40 mph. A quick additional coating of snow occurred from these snow showers.||In the wake of the squalls and cold front, strong wind gusts on the order of 35 to around 50 mph developed, continuing into the evening hours before easing at or just before midnight.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents/spinouts.

Heavy rainfall (1.5 to 2.5 inches) and snowmelt led to the formation of a significant ice jam on the East Branch of the Ausable River at Au Sable Forks, NY during the late morning to early afternoon hours of 18 February, 2022. The jam moved downstream from the upper portions of the watershed in the Town of Jay, first creating high water near the juncture of US Route 9N and Stickney Bridge Road. The jam subsequently shifted downstream, lodging near the confluence of the East and West Branches of the Ausable River at Au Sable Forks. As a result, significant water was impounded behind the jam, severely flooding portions of the town, specifically on the Clinton County side of the river in what is called the Jersey section in the vicinity of Broad Street and Intervale Avenue. Flooding was also observed at the local Tops Supermarket and nearby Riverside Park. The river forecast gage on the East Branch of the Ausable River at Au Sable Forks (ASFN6) crested at 15.19 feet, its third highest crest on record. The jam held in place for approximately 2 to 3 hours before moving downstream, easing the flooding in town. Thereafter, it lodged shortly for a second time just upstream of the other Au Sable Forks gage (AUSN6) where flooding was noted along portions of Route 9N and some property on the south bank owned by Northline Utilities LLC. After a short time, the jam moved further downstream, finally lodging in the vicinity of the Lower Road Bridge near the high school in Clintonville, NY. Subsequent flooding of Dugway and Cassidy Roads on the south bank occurred before water carved a path through the jam and high water concerns eased by mid-afternoon. Post-event coordination with Clinton and Essex County Emergency Management revealed 35 homes and 1 town owned property damaged. Of those, 7 were deemed to have received critical damage, with 2 buildings and 4 apartments condemned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant ice jam formed on the East Branch of the Ausable River at Au Sable Forks, NY during the late morning to early afternoon hours of 18 February, 2022. The jam moved downstream from the upper portions of the watershed in the Town of Jay, first creating high water near the juncture of US Route 9N and Stickney Bridge Road. The jam subsequently shifted downstream, lodging near the confluence of the East and West Branches of the Ausable River at Au Sable Forks. As a result, significant water was impounded behind the jam, severely flooding portions of the town, specifically on the Essex County side of the river in what is called the Jersey section in the vicinity of Broad Street and Intervale Avenue. Flooding was also observed on the Clinton County side at the local Tops Supermarket and nearby Riverside Park shown here. The river forecast gage on the East Branch of the Ausable River at Au Sable Forks (ASFN6) crested at 15.19 feet, its third highest crest on record. The jam held in place for approximately 2 to 3 hours before moving downstream, easing the flooding in town. Post-event coordination with Clinton and Essex County Emergency Management revealed 35 homes and 1 town owned property damaged on the Essex county side. Of those, 7 were deemed to have received critical damage, with 2 buildings and 4 apartments condemned.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Howard Johnson by Wyndham Lake George hotel parking lot on Canada Street was flooded early Friday morning. The Lake George Volunteer Fire Department rescued a man who was trapped on top of his pickup truck in the flooded parking lot. Lake George Fire Chief said the flooding was a result of water runoff from Prospect Mountain that flows into the drainage portion of the parking lot which wasn't able to keep up with the amount of water coming in. There was about 8 feet of water in the parking lot. The rooms on the ground level of the hotel also flooded with water.

An arctic front draped across northern NY and the VT-Canadian border on February 3rd slowly drifted south across much of NY and the VT/MA border by late in the day of February 4th. Light precipitation moved across northern NY during the morning and early afternoon hours of February 3rd with primarily snow in the St. Lawrence River valley and the Adirondacks' with light rain to light snow in the Champlain Valley.||However, during the late evening and overnight hours of February 3rd into the midday hours of February 4th, a series of weak low pressure areas moved northeast along the aforementioned boundary and brought steadier, heavier precipitation in the form of snow across northern NY, especially the Adirondacks and Champlain Valley. ||Storm total snowfall ranged from 6 to 10 inches across the St. Lawrence valley to 10 to 16 inches across the Adirondacks and the Champlain Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall ranged from 10 to 14 inches with much of this falling in the late evening of February 3rd to around daybreak on February 4th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam from the East Canada Creek resulted in flooding along portions of Van Buren Street and Dolge Avenue in the Village of Dolgeville. Homeowners had to evacuate from at least 10 homes due to the flooding. One of the residents reported chunks of ice knocking trees down along its path and flooding impacting some garages and basements. Flood waters began to recede later in the evening with crews still working into the overnight hours to get the water flowing back into the channel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast along the Tennessee Valley and the Lower Appalachains and redeveloped along the Mid Atlantic coast as it moved northeast. A brief mix of sleet and snow changed to freezing rain, and eventually plain rain before ending. Snow amounts were fairly negligible except in far northern and northwestern areas where approximately a couple of inches of snow and sleet fell before the change to freezing rain and even plain rain in most places. No impact LSRs were issued / reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported 0.11 inches of ice accretion in Manhasset Hills.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall ranged from 12 to 16 inches with much of this falling in the late evening of February 3rd to around daybreak on February 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure developed along a frontal wave along the southeast coastline and moved northeast offshore as 700 mb frontogenesis contributed to sub advisory and advisory level snowfall across Southeastern New York. Impacts were minimal as the snow was wet in nature and had difficulty sticking on cement surfaces. Not all zones are entered as with a warm ground some zones had under 2.5 inches of accumulated snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2.6 inches at 1 SSE Spring Valley and the public reported 2.3 inches in Nyack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system pushed across the region during February 17-18, 2022. Warm air pushed northward into the region along with periods of rainfall before a cold front brought the return of windy and colder weather. A convective line of gusty showers also accompanied the cold frontal passage. Some locations picked up over 1 inch of rainfall which led to minor flooding in a few locations. ||In the wake of the cold front, strong winds affected areas mainly along and south of I-90 with gusts 40 to 55 mph observed, resulted in a few downed trees. Some power outages occurred as a result of the wind, mainly across Ulster County.||The warm and rainy weather ahead of the cold front resulted in some snow melt as well as some ice to break up on area rivers and streams. As the colder weather returned, localized ice jams formed, some of which led to flooding, including in Dolgeville and Canajoharie.

An area of low pressure pushed across the region on Saturday, February 19, 2022. A passing warm front brought a period of light snow to the region during the morning and early afternoon hours with mainly a coating to two inches of accumulation. This snow was enough to cause a few vehicle spinouts/accidents, especially along interstates 87 and 90.||In the wake of this light snow, snow showers and squalls developed ahead of a cold front and pushed eastward during the afternoon hours, briefly but rapidly reducing visibility to at or less than one quarter of a mile at times with occasional wind gusts between 25 and 40 mph. A quick additional coating of snow occurred from these snow showers.||In the wake of the squalls and cold front, strong wind gusts on the order of 35 to around 50 mph developed, continuing into the evening hours before easing at or just before midnight.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents/spinouts.

An area of low pressure moved across the region bringing mild and windy weather along with periods of rainfall, some heavy enough, to cause localized areas of flooding. An ice jam also developed in the Village of Dolgeville leading to flooding along the East Canada Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam blocking the Sacandaga River broke, causing an increase in water downstream and flooded multiple homes along River Road in the Town of Hope. Four individuals and one dog were rescued by New York State Forest Rangers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region during the morning and early afternoon hours on Friday, February 25, 2022 producing a period of snow, heavy at times, with sleet and freezing rain mixing in at times for portions of the eastern Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from as low as 2 to 4 inches (common in parts of the mid-Hudson Valley) to as much as 6 to 9 inches elsewhere. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the morning rush hour with snowfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour at that time, resulting in significant impacts including several vehicle accidents with some minor injuries reported.||The New York State Thruway reduced its speed limit to 45 mph during the early morning hours due to the difficult travel conditions. Many area colleges and universities cancelled or delayed classes. Dozens of outgoing and incoming flights were cancelled at the Albany International Airport. ||The snowstorm led to the issuance of snow emergencies for some municipalities including Kingston, Catskill, Athens, Greenport, Cairo and Chatham.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, flight cancellations, school closures.

An area of low pressure pushed across the region on Saturday, February 19, 2022. A passing warm front brought a period of light snow to the region during the morning and early afternoon hours with mainly a coating to two inches of accumulation. This snow was enough to cause a few vehicle spinouts/accidents, especially along interstates 87 and 90.||In the wake of this light snow, snow showers and squalls developed ahead of a cold front and pushed eastward during the afternoon hours, briefly but rapidly reducing visibility to at or less than one quarter of a mile at times with occasional wind gusts between 25 and 40 mph. A quick additional coating of snow occurred from these snow showers.||In the wake of the squalls and cold front, strong wind gusts on the order of 35 to around 50 mph developed, continuing into the evening hours before easing at or just before midnight.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents/spinouts.

A frontal boundary slowly sagged southward through the area. This allowed for deep cold air to make its way south of the Pennsylvania state line. A series of weak disturbances then worked down this front bringing several rounds of moderate to heavy snow. The heaviest snow fell in the evening of February 3 for most areas. This occurred after a slow changeover from rain to snow as the front sagged southward. By the end of the event, many portions of the area received more than a foot of snow. Selected reports include 16.5 inches in Lockport, 12.5 inches in Kenmore, 12.3 inches in Gerry, 16.8 inches in Perrysburg, 13.6 inches in Sardinia, 10.2 inches in Medina, 15 inches in Webster, 12 inches in Batavia, 13 inches in Attica, 12.6 inches in Dansville, 10.8 inches in Alfred, 14 inches in Marion, 11.5 inches in Fulton, 8.3 inches in Watertown, 11.5 inches in Constableville, and 9.2 inches in Bristol Harbor.
