EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed along a sea breeze boundary across Long Island. One of the thunderstorms was able to become severe and caused damaging winds as well as large hail in Central Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture of quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed along a sea breeze boundary across Long Island. One of the thunderstorms was able to become severe and caused damaging winds as well as large hail in Central Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires down in flames on Middle Country Road and South Coleman Road in Centereach NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent clipper passed to the north of the region, producing a period of snow squalls and  gusty winds along the clipper cold front.  Lake enhanced snow quickly followed the cold front to the east of Lake Ontario. This was due to rapidly deepening cold air that developed that increased lake-induced instability to a very deep depth.  This allowed for a brief period of lake effect and upslope flow onto the Tug Hill, where low-end warning snowfall amounts were received. The snowfall reports received from the very short event included 8 inches in New Bremen, 8.6 inches in Constableville, and 7.2 inches in Redfield.

Surface low pressure over southeast Ohio tracked northeast into western New York. Large scale ascent from an approaching shortwave trough with an upper level dual jet structure overhead aided in deepening the surface low. The low was tracking quickly as it transitioned to a larger coastal storm. Warm air advection ahead of the low led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain across western New York. The corridor of sleet and freezing rain reached as far north as the Niagara Frontier and Lower Genesee Valley. As the surface low moved into central New York, any sleet or freezing rain transitioned to snow. Another area of snow across Michigan moved into western New York behind the first round, however additional accumulations from this were inconsequential. The heaviest snow totals were in the North Country and include 7.1 inches in Natural Bridge, 8.8 inches in Mexico, and 8 inches in Lowville.

An area of low pressure pushed across the region on Saturday, February 19, 2022. A passing warm front brought a period of light snow to the region during the morning and early afternoon hours with mainly a coating to two inches of accumulation. This snow was enough to cause a few vehicle spinouts/accidents, especially along interstates 87 and 90.||In the wake of this light snow, snow showers and squalls developed ahead of a cold front and pushed eastward during the afternoon hours, briefly but rapidly reducing visibility to at or less than one quarter of a mile at times with occasional wind gusts between 25 and 40 mph. A quick additional coating of snow occurred from these snow showers.||In the wake of the squalls and cold front, strong wind gusts on the order of 35 to around 50 mph developed, continuing into the evening hours before easing at or just before midnight.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents/spinouts.

A storm system pushed across the region during February 17-18, 2022. Warm air pushed northward into the region along with periods of rainfall before a cold front brought the return of windy and colder weather. A convective line of gusty showers also accompanied the cold frontal passage. Some locations picked up over 1 inch of rainfall which led to minor flooding in a few locations. ||In the wake of the cold front, strong winds affected areas mainly along and south of I-90 with gusts 40 to 55 mph observed, resulted in a few downed trees. Some power outages occurred as a result of the wind, mainly across Ulster County.||The warm and rainy weather ahead of the cold front resulted in some snow melt as well as some ice to break up on area rivers and streams. As the colder weather returned, localized ice jams formed, some of which led to flooding, including in Dolgeville and Canajoharie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region during the morning and early afternoon hours on Friday, February 25, 2022 producing a period of snow, heavy at times, with sleet and freezing rain mixing in at times for portions of the eastern Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from as low as 2 to 4 inches (common in parts of the mid-Hudson Valley) to as much as 6 to 9 inches elsewhere. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the morning rush hour with snowfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour at that time, resulting in significant impacts including several vehicle accidents with some minor injuries reported.||The New York State Thruway reduced its speed limit to 45 mph during the early morning hours due to the difficult travel conditions. Many area colleges and universities cancelled or delayed classes. Dozens of outgoing and incoming flights were cancelled at the Albany International Airport. ||The snowstorm led to the issuance of snow emergencies for some municipalities including Kingston, Catskill, Athens, Greenport, Cairo and Chatham.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, flight cancellations, school closures.

Surface low pressure over southeast Ohio tracked northeast into western New York. Large scale ascent from an approaching shortwave trough with an upper level dual jet structure overhead aided in deepening the surface low. The low was tracking quickly as it transitioned to a larger coastal storm. Warm air advection ahead of the low led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain across western New York. The corridor of sleet and freezing rain reached as far north as the Niagara Frontier and Lower Genesee Valley. As the surface low moved into central New York, any sleet or freezing rain transitioned to snow. Another area of snow across Michigan moved into western New York behind the first round, however additional accumulations from this were inconsequential. The heaviest snow totals were in the North Country and include 7.1 inches in Natural Bridge, 8.8 inches in Mexico, and 8 inches in Lowville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex frontal system moved through the Central New York bringing snow and a wintry mix to the region. Several inches of snow were observed across the Finger Lakes and Mohawk Valley with the highest totals near 18. Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times. Lesser amounts were observed closer to the NY/PA border where the transition zone consisted of a snow/sleet mix. Isolated ice accumulations were reported in the higher elevations in the transition zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet accumulated 1 to 4 inches. Ice accumulated 0.3 to 0.4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front slowly moved from northwest to southeast across northern NY during the late evening of February 17th and overnight hours of February 18th, bringing much colder air. Meanwhile, a weak surface wave was traveling northeast along this boundary. The combination allowed for light rain to change to freezing rain and eventually sleet and snow through the morning hours of February 18th.||Ice accumulations were generally around a tenth (0.10) of an inch with sleet/snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches that created hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Combined snow and sleet accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common along with a tenth of an inch of ice (freezing rain) accumulation. These conditions led to hazardous driving across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed along the Hudson River in the Town of Thurman causing water and ice to flow over Route 418 (River Street) between River Road and Hickory Hill Road. Water began to flow over the road during the evening hours on Saturday, February 19 causing the road to close. The water and ice eventually receded on Monday, February 28, allowing the road to reopen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam led to flooding on Route 418 (River Street) between Hickory Hill Road and River Road in the Town of Thurman.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Standing water and minor flooding was reported around Hickory Hill Road and Route 5 near the Village of Mohawk.

An arctic front slowly moved from northwest to southeast across northern NY during the late evening of February 17th and overnight hours of February 18th, bringing much colder air. Meanwhile, a weak surface wave was traveling northeast along this boundary. The combination allowed for light rain to change to freezing rain and eventually sleet and snow through the morning hours of February 18th.||Ice accumulations were generally around a tenth (0.10) of an inch with sleet/snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches that created hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to freezing rain and accumulated to a tenth of an inch or less, followed by 1/2 to 1 inch of sleet then snowfall accumulating 2-4 inches created hazardous driving conditions.

A complex frontal system moved through the Central New York bringing snow and a wintry mix to the region. Several inches of snow were observed across the Finger Lakes and Mohawk Valley with the highest totals near 18. Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour at times. Lesser amounts were observed closer to the NY/PA border where the transition zone consisted of a snow/sleet mix. Isolated ice accumulations were reported in the higher elevations in the transition zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 10 to 14 inches.

An area of low pressure pushed across the region during the morning and early afternoon hours on Friday, February 25, 2022 producing a period of snow, heavy at times, with sleet and freezing rain mixing in at times for portions of the eastern Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from as low as 2 to 4 inches (common in parts of the mid-Hudson Valley) to as much as 6 to 9 inches elsewhere. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the morning rush hour with snowfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour at that time, resulting in significant impacts including several vehicle accidents with some minor injuries reported.||The New York State Thruway reduced its speed limit to 45 mph during the early morning hours due to the difficult travel conditions. Many area colleges and universities cancelled or delayed classes. Dozens of outgoing and incoming flights were cancelled at the Albany International Airport. ||The snowstorm led to the issuance of snow emergencies for some municipalities including Kingston, Catskill, Athens, Greenport, Cairo and Chatham.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, flight cancellations, school closures.

The large scale weather pattern shifted greatly beyond the first week of January such that Arctic air was migrating into the Great Lakes after a fairly warm start to the winter. This left almost all of the Great Lakes ice free coming into the period, and as a result, while most of western New York was quite cold, attenuation of the Arctic air was an ongoing process for most areas due to extensive open water and warming from the Great Lakes. This wasn't the case, however, in the North Country, where Jefferson, Lewis, and occasionally even Oswego counties experienced nearly nightly sub-zero temperatures with multiple incidents of wind chill values below -30 degrees Fahrenheit. On January 11, this included Alexandria Bay and Copenhagen at -30. On January 15, this included Croghan at -53, Watertown at -31, Fort Drum at -33, and Osceola at -48.

An area of low pressure pushed across the region during the morning and early afternoon hours on Friday, February 25, 2022 producing a period of snow, heavy at times, with sleet and freezing rain mixing in at times for portions of the eastern Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from as low as 2 to 4 inches (common in parts of the mid-Hudson Valley) to as much as 6 to 9 inches elsewhere. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the morning rush hour with snowfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour at that time, resulting in significant impacts including several vehicle accidents with some minor injuries reported.||The New York State Thruway reduced its speed limit to 45 mph during the early morning hours due to the difficult travel conditions. Many area colleges and universities cancelled or delayed classes. Dozens of outgoing and incoming flights were cancelled at the Albany International Airport. ||The snowstorm led to the issuance of snow emergencies for some municipalities including Kingston, Catskill, Athens, Greenport, Cairo and Chatham.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, flight cancellations, school closures.

An area of low pressure pushed across the region on Saturday, February 19, 2022. A passing warm front brought a period of light snow to the region during the morning and early afternoon hours with mainly a coating to two inches of accumulation. This snow was enough to cause a few vehicle spinouts/accidents, especially along interstates 87 and 90.||In the wake of this light snow, snow showers and squalls developed ahead of a cold front and pushed eastward during the afternoon hours, briefly but rapidly reducing visibility to at or less than one quarter of a mile at times with occasional wind gusts between 25 and 40 mph. A quick additional coating of snow occurred from these snow showers.||In the wake of the squalls and cold front, strong wind gusts on the order of 35 to around 50 mph developed, continuing into the evening hours before easing at or just before midnight.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents/spinouts.

A prolific winter storm began on Thursday, February 3 and continued into Friday, February 4. Precipitation began as rain for most areas on February 3 with the exception of the Adirondacks which began as a mix of rain and snow. Colder air gradually pushed southward into the overnight and early morning hours of February 4 resulting in a changeover to a wintry mix of sleet, freezing rain and snow. Most locations picked up 1 to 2 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation from this event with the majority of it falling as one or multiple types of frozen precipitation. Precipitation ended during the afternoon hours on February 4.||Portions of the western Mohawk Valley and Adirondacks into portions of far northern Washington County saw mostly heavy snow from this event with accumulations ranging from 9 to 18 inches. The remainder of the Mohawk Valley into the Lake George Saratoga Region had mostly a combination of snow and sleet. Storm total snow and sleet amounts in this zone were in the 4 to 9 inch range. The Schoharie Valley into the Capital District and northern Taconics had an initial period of freezing rain before transitioning to a prolonged period of sleet, then ended as a period of light snow. Flat ice accumulations of 0.10 to 0.25 of an inch were observed followed by 1 to 2 inches of sleet and then 1 to 2 inches of snow. Much of the eastern Catskills, mid-Hudson Valley and central and southern Taconics remained rain for a longer period of time but gradually changed over to a prolonged period of freezing rain before ending as light snow and sleet. Flat ice amounts between 0.25 to 0.75 of an inch were common across the region with snow and sleet amounts of 2 inches or less.||Eastern Ulster County was the hardest hit with widespread damage due to ice accretion including downed trees and power lines and widespread power outages, some of which lasted for days after the event. A handful of other customers in parts of Columbia and Rensselaer counties also lost power.||Utility companies noted that more than 50,000 customers lost power from the storm across the Hudson Valley, most of them in eastern Ulster County. At least 44 utility poles broke and more than 900 power lines came down as a result of the snow and ice with more than 500 individual damage locations. Several hundred electric line and tree cleaning professionals were called in to help clear roads from debris and restore power, some coming in from Indiana and Pennsylvania. Ulster County executive Pat Ryan declared a weather-related state of emergency for the area. The widespread power outages prompted the opening of warming centers and the distribution of dry ice. Members of the National Guard came in to assist in recovery operations. Cleanup efforts in Kingston cost the city approximately $100,000 according to lawmakers.||State Police reported multiple fallen trees along the New York State Thruway (Interstate 87 southbound) between exits 19 and 20, blocking the right lane of the highway, in Ulster County.||States of Emergencies went into effect for other jurisdictions as well including some in southern Herkimer County. ||The winter storm led to a 45 mph speed limit restriction to go into effect for the New York State Thruway. Several schools and businesses closed on Friday, February 4 as a result of the storm. Most flights at Albany International Airport were canceled.||Key impacts: travel delays, tree and wire damage, highway crashes, flight cancellations, school closures, road closures, power outages.

Colder air gradually pushed southward into the overnight and early morning hours of February 4 resulting in a changeover to a wintry mix of sleet, freezing rain and snow. Most locations picked up 1 to 2 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation from this event with the majority of it falling as one or multiple types of frozen precipitation. Precipitation ended during the afternoon hours on February 4.||Portions of the western Mohawk Valley and Adirondacks into portions of far northern Washington County saw mostly heavy snow from this event with accumulations ranging from 9 to 18 inches. The remainder of the Mohawk Valley into the Lake George Saratoga Region had mostly a combination of snow and sleet. Storm total snow and sleet amounts in this zone were in the 4 to 9 inch range. The Schoharie Valley into the Capital District and northern Taconics had an initial period of freezing rain before transitioning to a prolonged period of sleet, then ended as a period of light snow. Flat ice accumulations of 0.10 to 0.25 of an inch were observed followed by 1 to 2 inches of sleet and then 1 to 2 inches of snow. Much of the eastern Catskills, mid-Hudson Valley and central and southern Taconics remained rain for a longer period of time but gradually changed over to a prolonged period of freezing rain before ending as light snow and sleet. Flat ice amounts between 0.25 to 0.75 of an inch were common across the region with snow and sleet amounts of 2 inches or less.||Eastern Ulster County was the hardest hit with widespread damage due to ice accretion including downed trees and power lines and widespread power outages, some of which lasted for days after the event. A handful of other customers in parts of Columbia and Rensselaer counties also lost power.||Utility companies noted that more than 50,000 customers lost power from the storm across the Hudson Valley, most of them in eastern Ulster County. At least 44 utility poles broke and more than 900 power lines came down as a result of the snow and ice with more than 500 individual damage locations. Several hundred electric line and tree cleaning professionals were called in to help clear roads from debris and restore power, some coming in from Indiana and Pennsylvania. Ulster County executive Pat Ryan declared a weather-related state of emergency for the area. The widespread power outages prompted the opening of warming centers and the distribution of dry ice. Members of the National Guard came in to assist in recovery operations. Cleanup efforts in Kingston cost the city approximately $100,000 according to lawmakers.||State Police reported multiple fallen trees along the New York State Thruway (Interstate 87 southbound) between exits 19 and 20, blocking the right lane of the highway, in Ulster County.||States of Emergencies went into effect for other jurisdictions as well including some in southern Herkimer County. ||The winter storm led to a 45 mph speed limit restriction to go into effect for the New York State Thruway. Several schools and businesses closed on Friday, February 4 as a result of the storm. Most flights at Albany International Airport were canceled.||Key impacts: travel delays, tree and wire damage, highway crashes, flight cancellations, school closures, road closures, power outages.

Low pressure tracked from the upper Great Lakes to Quebec with a trailing cold front crossing the region. Unlike in most high wind events, the strong winds from this system occurred before the cold frontal passage with mixing of a 80 kt low level jet due to the strong March sunshine. Because of the timing as well, and with a warm air mass in place, the strongest winds occurred away from the Great Lakes, where cold surface water reduced instability in the lowest levels and thus reduced wind gusts near the lakeshore areas. Selected wind gust reports included 68 mph at Elma Center, 58 mph at the Buffalo-Niagara International Airport, 66 mph in Batavia, 72 mph at the Rochester Airport, 64 mph at the Niagara Falls Airport, 58 mph at Clifton Springs, 64 mph at Medina, 58 mph at Ontario, 59 mph at Wyoming, at 58 mph at the East Aurora mesonet site.

A cold front was to our west and stalled out. A highly unstable air mass in place. Two rounds of severe weather occurred. The first was over the North Country and St. Lawrence Valley and aided by a well-developed vortex that had traversed the Toronto area early in the afternoon. The lake breeze off of Lake Erie protected the Buffalo metro area, but it ultimately interacted with the lake breeze and instability to the east of Lake Ontario. These storms produced mostly large hail. A second round of severe storms occurred later in the evening as a couple of passing waves triggered thunderstorms due to very high instability. Wind damage and heavy rain with some flooding were the main issues.  Most of this was from the Genesee Valley to the west as thunderstorms weakened once they pushed eastward after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across western New York triggering scattered thunderstorms over the northern Finger Lakes late in the morning. As these thunderstorms increased, a prolific amount of lightning occurred. The line then swept east across the North Country to the east of Lake Ontario into the afternoon producing straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and lines were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the upper levels a negatively tilted trough approached from the west as associated surface low pressure formed and deepened as it passed to the north and west of the CWA. A strong cold front extending down from the surface low moved through the area allowing for severe thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires down on a home on Goodridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed along a pre-frontal trough just ahead of the a cold front in PA and NY. The storms headed east and moved through southeastern NJ bringing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch tree limbs broken and shingles blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region during the morning and early afternoon hours on Friday, February 25, 2022 producing a period of snow, heavy at times, with sleet and freezing rain mixing in at times for portions of the eastern Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from as low as 2 to 4 inches (common in parts of the mid-Hudson Valley) to as much as 6 to 9 inches elsewhere. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the morning rush hour with snowfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour at that time, resulting in significant impacts including several vehicle accidents with some minor injuries reported.||The New York State Thruway reduced its speed limit to 45 mph during the early morning hours due to the difficult travel conditions. Many area colleges and universities cancelled or delayed classes. Dozens of outgoing and incoming flights were cancelled at the Albany International Airport. ||The snowstorm led to the issuance of snow emergencies for some municipalities including Kingston, Catskill, Athens, Greenport, Cairo and Chatham.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, flight cancellations, school closures.

The line then swept east across the North Country to the east of Lake Ontario into the afternoon producing straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and lines were downed across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure pushed across the region during the morning and early afternoon hours on Friday, February 25, 2022 producing a period of snow, heavy at times, with sleet and freezing rain mixing in at times for portions of the eastern Catskills and mid-Hudson Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from as low as 2 to 4 inches (common in parts of the mid-Hudson Valley) to as much as 6 to 9 inches elsewhere. The heaviest snow fell at the time of the morning rush hour with snowfall rates in excess of 1 inch per hour at that time, resulting in significant impacts including several vehicle accidents with some minor injuries reported.||The New York State Thruway reduced its speed limit to 45 mph during the early morning hours due to the difficult travel conditions. Many area colleges and universities cancelled or delayed classes. Dozens of outgoing and incoming flights were cancelled at the Albany International Airport. ||The snowstorm led to the issuance of snow emergencies for some municipalities including Kingston, Catskill, Athens, Greenport, Cairo and Chatham.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, flight cancellations, school closures.

A nearly stationary band of lake-effect snow produced snowfall amounts of 7 to 9 inches across portions of northern Herkimer and Hamilton counties during the morning and early afternoon hours on Sunday, February 28, 2022. An approaching cold front with snow showers brought an end to the event.

Low pressure tracked from the upper Great Lakes to Quebec with a trailing cold front crossing the region. Unlike in most high wind events, the strong winds from this system occurred before the cold frontal passage with mixing of a 80 kt low level jet due to the strong March sunshine. Because of the timing as well, and with a warm air mass in place, the strongest winds occurred away from the Great Lakes, where cold surface water reduced instability in the lowest levels and thus reduced wind gusts near the lakeshore areas. Selected wind gust reports included 68 mph at Elma Center, 58 mph at the Buffalo-Niagara International Airport, 66 mph in Batavia, 72 mph at the Rochester Airport, 64 mph at the Niagara Falls Airport, 58 mph at Clifton Springs, 64 mph at Medina, 58 mph at Ontario, 59 mph at Wyoming, at 58 mph at the East Aurora mesonet site.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 year old man was killed when a tree fell on him in Bergen at approximately 2 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and surface cold front approach from the west sparking a line of severe thunderstorms that moved through the area with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site MHRN6 2 miles SEof Elizabeth NJ measured a 52 knot wind gust.

A cold front swept through Central New York, triggering thunderstorms along the boundary. The environment was favorable for severe weather and a couple isolated storms did have strong wind gusts. Most of the damage included large trees knocked over onto roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree and wires down on Farm to Market Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave (mid-level disturbance) and a cold front moved into a marginal/moderate unstable airmass across northern NY during the afternoon of May 16th. Numerous showers and some scattered thunderstorms developed with a few storms producing severe weather in terms of localized damaging winds and/or hail of one inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported with estimated hail size of a quarter (one-inch in diameter). In addition, strong winds knocked down a few trees.

A cold front was to our west and stalled out. A highly unstable air mass in place. Two rounds of severe weather occurred. The first was over the North Country and St. Lawrence Valley and aided by a well-developed vortex that had traversed the Toronto area early in the afternoon. The lake breeze off of Lake Erie protected the Buffalo metro area, but it ultimately interacted with the lake breeze and instability to the east of Lake Ontario. These storms produced mostly large hail. A second round of severe storms occurred later in the evening as a couple of passing waves triggered thunderstorms due to very high instability. Wind damage and heavy rain with some flooding were the main issues.  Most of this was from the Genesee Valley to the west as thunderstorms weakened once they pushed eastward after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported on a portion of Plank and Corry Roads south of Clymer.

A prolific winter storm began on Thursday, February 3 and continued into Friday, February 4. Precipitation began as rain for most areas on February 3 with the exception of the Adirondacks which began as a mix of rain and snow. Colder air gradually pushed southward into the overnight and early morning hours of February 4 resulting in a changeover to a wintry mix of sleet, freezing rain and snow. Most locations picked up 1 to 2 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation from this event with the majority of it falling as one or multiple types of frozen precipitation. Precipitation ended during the afternoon hours on February 4.||Portions of the western Mohawk Valley and Adirondacks into portions of far northern Washington County saw mostly heavy snow from this event with accumulations ranging from 9 to 18 inches. The remainder of the Mohawk Valley into the Lake George Saratoga Region had mostly a combination of snow and sleet. Storm total snow and sleet amounts in this zone were in the 4 to 9 inch range. The Schoharie Valley into the Capital District and northern Taconics had an initial period of freezing rain before transitioning to a prolonged period of sleet, then ended as a period of light snow. Flat ice accumulations of 0.10 to 0.25 of an inch were observed followed by 1 to 2 inches of sleet and then 1 to 2 inches of snow. Much of the eastern Catskills, mid-Hudson Valley and central and southern Taconics remained rain for a longer period of time but gradually changed over to a prolonged period of freezing rain before ending as light snow and sleet. Flat ice amounts between 0.25 to 0.75 of an inch were common across the region with snow and sleet amounts of 2 inches or less.||Eastern Ulster County was the hardest hit with widespread damage due to ice accretion including downed trees and power lines and widespread power outages, some of which lasted for days after the event. A handful of other customers in parts of Columbia and Rensselaer counties also lost power.||Utility companies noted that more than 50,000 customers lost power from the storm across the Hudson Valley, most of them in eastern Ulster County. At least 44 utility poles broke and more than 900 power lines came down as a result of the snow and ice with more than 500 individual damage locations. Several hundred electric line and tree cleaning professionals were called in to help clear roads from debris and restore power, some coming in from Indiana and Pennsylvania. Ulster County executive Pat Ryan declared a weather-related state of emergency for the area. The widespread power outages prompted the opening of warming centers and the distribution of dry ice. Members of the National Guard came in to assist in recovery operations. Cleanup efforts in Kingston cost the city approximately $100,000 according to lawmakers.||State Police reported multiple fallen trees along the New York State Thruway (Interstate 87 southbound) between exits 19 and 20, blocking the right lane of the highway, in Ulster County.||States of Emergencies went into effect for other jurisdictions as well including some in southern Herkimer County. ||The winter storm led to a 45 mph speed limit restriction to go into effect for the New York State Thruway. Several schools and businesses closed on Friday, February 4 as a result of the storm. Most flights at Albany International Airport were canceled.||Key impacts: travel delays, tree and wire damage, highway crashes, flight cancellations, school closures, road closures, power outages.

Low pressure tracked from the upper Great Lakes to Quebec with a trailing cold front crossing the region. Unlike in most high wind events, the strong winds from this system occurred before the cold frontal passage with mixing of a 80 kt low level jet due to the strong March sunshine. Because of the timing as well, and with a warm air mass in place, the strongest winds occurred away from the Great Lakes, where cold surface water reduced instability in the lowest levels and thus reduced wind gusts near the lakeshore areas. Selected wind gust reports included 68 mph at Elma Center, 58 mph at the Buffalo-Niagara International Airport, 66 mph in Batavia, 72 mph at the Rochester Airport, 64 mph at the Niagara Falls Airport, 58 mph at Clifton Springs, 64 mph at Medina, 58 mph at Ontario, 59 mph at Wyoming, at 58 mph at the East Aurora mesonet site.

A cold front was to our west and stalled out. A highly unstable air mass in place. Two rounds of severe weather occurred. The first was over the North Country and St. Lawrence Valley and aided by a well-developed vortex that had traversed the Toronto area early in the afternoon. The lake breeze off of Lake Erie protected the Buffalo metro area, but it ultimately interacted with the lake breeze and instability to the east of Lake Ontario. These storms produced mostly large hail. A second round of severe storms occurred later in the evening as a couple of passing waves triggered thunderstorms due to very high instability. Wind damage and heavy rain with some flooding were the main issues.  Most of this was from the Genesee Valley to the west as thunderstorms weakened once they pushed eastward after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto Longview Road.

Low pressure tracked from the upper Great Lakes to Quebec with a trailing cold front crossing the region. Unlike in most high wind events, the strong winds from this system occurred before the cold frontal passage with mixing of a 80 kt low level jet due to the strong March sunshine. Because of the timing as well, and with a warm air mass in place, the strongest winds occurred away from the Great Lakes, where cold surface water reduced instability in the lowest levels and thus reduced wind gusts near the lakeshore areas. Selected wind gust reports included 68 mph at Elma Center, 58 mph at the Buffalo-Niagara International Airport, 66 mph in Batavia, 72 mph at the Rochester Airport, 64 mph at the Niagara Falls Airport, 58 mph at Clifton Springs, 64 mph at Medina, 58 mph at Ontario, 59 mph at Wyoming, at 58 mph at the East Aurora mesonet site.

A cold front was to our west and stalled out. A highly unstable air mass in place. Two rounds of severe weather occurred. The first was over the North Country and St. Lawrence Valley and aided by a well-developed vortex that had traversed the Toronto area early in the afternoon. The lake breeze off of Lake Erie protected the Buffalo metro area, but it ultimately interacted with the lake breeze and instability to the east of Lake Ontario. These storms produced mostly large hail. A second round of severe storms occurred later in the evening as a couple of passing waves triggered thunderstorms due to very high instability. Wind damage and heavy rain with some flooding were the main issues.  Most of this was from the Genesee Valley to the west as thunderstorms weakened once they pushed eastward after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Lyndon Center Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A double barreled storm system with copious amounts of moisture and marginal freezing temperatures moved across New England and New York on April 19th during the early to mid-morning hours. In the higher elevations of northern NY, especially the Adirondacks, a heavy wet snow caked everything and accumulated 6 to 12 inches and locally higher, resulting in numerous tree limb/branch and some tree damage and numerous power outages.  Also, the intensity of the precipitation allowed cold air to lower to the surface in the Champlain Valley and this accounted for 4 to 8 inches of snow accumulation and similar impacts. An estimated 25,000 customers lost power in NY's four northern counties and more than 200,000 customers statewide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changes to a heavy, wet pasty snow during the early morning hours and quickly accumulated, especially the higher elevations. Snowfall accumulations of 6 to 15+ inches occurred and resulted in downed tree limbs, branches and trees that caused significant  power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front advanced slowly towards western New York in the afternoon and evening with convection focused along a pre-frontal trough and an outflow boundary ahead of the main cold front.  Large scale support was weak, with the mid-level trough and upper level jet lagging well behind the cold front, which yielded the bulk of the large scale ascent above the cold, post-frontal air. A broken line of convection developed over northern Ohio and lasted for a number of hours as it crossed northeast Ohio, northwest Pennsylvania, and eventually entered western New York.  A very narrow axis of modest instability developed just ahead of the line and advected east into western New York. Storms that fired harnessed this instability as it intersected the lake breeze boundary to the east of Lake Erie. This allowed for multiple bowing segments to form over the western Southern Tier. These cells eventually were able to move east of the lake breeze boundary and formed a quasi-linear convective system that advanced toward the Genesee Valley. Multiple reports of wind damage were received, and a NWS storm survey confirmed a tornado in the Town of Alexander in Genesee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Northeast bringing periods of heavy rain to central New York, particularly the Upper Delaware river basin. This resulted in minor to moderate flooding of the headwater tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall led to excessive runoff into the East Branch Delaware River below the Pepacton Reservoir. The stream gage located near Harvard, NY reached a crest of 12.76 feet on April 8th at 2:30 PM. This is considered to be moderate flooding. Several roads were closed in the area of the stream gage, including NY SR 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy thunderstorms developed in the warm sector of a frontal system on July 18th, 2022. These thunderstorms developed in an environment with 2 to 2.3 inch PWAT values and CAPE near 3000 J/kg. Training thunderstorms produced a widespread 1 to 3 inches of rainfall with localized spots up to 4 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closure both directions on Bronx River Parkway between exit Sprain Brook Parkway/Yonkers and Exit 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful storm across Ontario (972mb) remained nearly stationary from the evening of December 23rd (Friday) through the morning of December 25th (Sunday). An arctic front associated with this storm moved across Lake Ontario and the St. Lawrence valley during the early afternoon hours of December 23rd but the favored unidirectional SW flow across Lake Ontario didn't establish until Friday evening.||Friday evening through early Sunday morning, a very strong southwest flow of 40 to 50 knots across Lake Ontario led to blizzard conditions across southwest St. Lawrence county through Saturday night and near blizzard conditions at times across northern St. Lawrence county, mainly along/west of Route 11. The lake effect band shifted southeast during Sunday morning, bringing heavy lake effect snow to southeastern St. Lawrence county through Sunday evening.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 20 to 38 inches in Gouverneur, 20 inches in Morristown, 16 inches n West Fowler, 15 inches in Star Lake and 10 inches in South Colton with 6 to 15 inches across northern St. Lawrence along/west of Route 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and Travel Emergencies were declared for St. Lawrence county with northern St. Lawrence county west of Route 11, closer to Route 37 witnessing blizzard conditions at times with near zero visibilities, sustained winds 20 to 30 mph and frequent gusts in excess of 40 mph. Snowfall totals were 8 to 15+ inches with the higher amounts west of Route 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very powerful storm across Ontario (972mb) remained nearly stationary from the evening of December 23rd (Friday) through the morning of December 25th (Sunday). An arctic front associated with this storm moved across Lake Ontario and the St. Lawrence valley during the early afternoon hours of December 23rd but the favored unidirectional SW flow across Lake Ontario didn't establish until Friday evening.||Friday evening through early Sunday morning, a very strong southwest flow of 40 to 50 knots across Lake Ontario led to blizzard conditions across southwest St. Lawrence county through Saturday night and near blizzard conditions at times across northern St. Lawrence county, mainly along/west of Route 11. The lake effect band shifted southeast during Sunday morning, bringing heavy lake effect snow to southeastern St. Lawrence county through Sunday evening.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 20 to 38 inches in Gouverneur, 20 inches in Morristown, 16 inches n West Fowler, 15 inches in Star Lake and 10 inches in South Colton with 6 to 15 inches across northern St. Lawrence along/west of Route 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Synoptic snows produced 2 to 5 inches during the afternoon and evening of December 23rd with scattered snow showers on December 24th. The lake effect snow band rotated south across southeast St. Lawrence county on Christmas delivering 8 to 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and intense storm system tracked through the region from December 23-25, 2022. Heavy rain and snowmelt led to rises on the Raquette River in St. Lawrence County. Some ice breakup was noted, some of which affected portions of the Town of Pierrepoint, NY on the 25th where high water affected several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High flows on the Raquette River near the Hannawa Falls Dam led to ice breakup on December 25, 2022. Ice became jammed and impounded behind the Hannawa Falls Dam due to slowing flows which led to rapid rises in the river during late morning. Approximately four homes along T-Alexander Drive in the Town of Pierrepoint, NY where affected by high water to the basement and first floor levels, and were subsequently evacuated. No injuries were reported. Water receded by the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep mid-level trough crossed the region with an associated deep surface low that lifted northward across New England. Widespread and heaviest snow clipped Lewis County, while lake enhanced and upslope snow occurred east of both lakes. Little if any accumulating snowfall fell at the lower elevations due to marginal surface temperatures. Likewise, temperatures off the ground at 850 hPa were also only marginally supportive of snow. Between the synoptic and lake enhanced snow, amounts reached around 5 inches to the east of both lakes, however the overlay of synoptic, upslope, and lake enhancement allowed for much greater totals to the east of Lake Ontario. Reports of 19.8 inches in Highmark and 19.0 inches in Constableville were the most impressive in Lewis County. Tree damage in Lewis County was also extensive due to the water-laden nature of the snow and the sheer volume of snow on the trees. One spotter called conditions in Lewis County a ���total disaster��� due to ���lots of trees down��� and that they had ���no electricity, but there is internet.

A cold front advanced slowly towards western New York in the afternoon and evening with convection focused along a pre-frontal trough and an outflow boundary ahead of the main cold front.  Large scale support was weak, with the mid-level trough and upper level jet lagging well behind the cold front, which yielded the bulk of the large scale ascent above the cold, post-frontal air. A broken line of convection developed over northern Ohio and lasted for a number of hours as it crossed northeast Ohio, northwest Pennsylvania, and eventually entered western New York.  A very narrow axis of modest instability developed just ahead of the line and advected east into western New York. Storms that fired harnessed this instability as it intersected the lake breeze boundary to the east of Lake Erie. This allowed for multiple bowing segments to form over the western Southern Tier. These cells eventually were able to move east of the lake breeze boundary and formed a quasi-linear convective system that advanced toward the Genesee Valley. Multiple reports of wind damage were received, and a NWS storm survey confirmed a tornado in the Town of Alexander in Genesee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey team found that a tornado initially touched down just north of the Alexander Fire Department and Recreational Hall with damage to the roof of a pavilion. A nearby building had its metal roof peeled back and a trailer, with some supporting anchor braces, was rolled over on its side. The tornado continued east-northeast snapping numerous trees in a wooded area just to the west of NYS Route 98/Alexander Road. The tornado then removed shingles and shattered windows to residences along Alexander Road then produced tree damage in a convergent pattern to the east just before crossing Tonawanda Creek. Finally, the tornado crossed a field before snapping two very large trees at the Alexander Cemetery and an adjacent commercial building. This is where the tornado damage ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A double barreled storm system with copious amounts of moisture and marginal freezing temperatures moved across New England and New York on April 19th during the early to mid-morning hours. In the higher elevations of northern NY, especially the Adirondacks, a heavy wet snow caked everything and accumulated 6 to 12 inches and locally higher, resulting in numerous tree limb/branch and some tree damage and numerous power outages.  Also, the intensity of the precipitation allowed cold air to lower to the surface in the Champlain Valley and this accounted for 4 to 8 inches of snow accumulation and similar impacts. An estimated 25,000 customers lost power in NY's four northern counties and more than 200,000 customers statewide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a heavy, wet pasty snow in the valleys just before daybreak as the intensity of the precipitation advected enough cold air toward the surface. Snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were observed and enough to cause downed limbs and branches that resulted in power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal system produced widespread heavy rainfall and flooding across eastern New York from April 7-8, 2022. Rainfall amounts between 1.50 and 3.00 inches were common, though a few localized areas received upwards of 4.50 inches. Periods of light to moderate rainfall began during the daytime hours on April 7 with the steadiest and heaviest rainfall arriving during the evening hours on April 7 into the overnight hours on April 8 before ending.||This heavy rainfall led to over a dozen rivers to exceed minor flood stage with a few rivers reaching moderate flood stage. For some, it took days after the event for waters to recede. ||In addition to the heavy rainfall and widespread flooding, strong winds developed across portions of the Taconics with peak gusts in the 35-50 mph range.||The combination of rain and wind led to only sporadic power outages. As a result of the heavy rain and flooding, a Travel Advisory was issued for Montgomery County during the overnight hours of April 7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads within the cities of Gloversville and Johnstown were closed due to flooding along with some basement flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front advanced slowly towards western New York in the afternoon and evening with convection focused along a pre-frontal trough and an outflow boundary ahead of the main cold front.  Large scale support was weak, with the mid-level trough and upper level jet lagging well behind the cold front, which yielded the bulk of the large scale ascent above the cold, post-frontal air. A broken line of convection developed over northern Ohio and lasted for a number of hours as it crossed northeast Ohio, northwest Pennsylvania, and eventually entered western New York.  A very narrow axis of modest instability developed just ahead of the line and advected east into western New York. Storms that fired harnessed this instability as it intersected the lake breeze boundary to the east of Lake Erie. This allowed for multiple bowing segments to form over the western Southern Tier. These cells eventually were able to move east of the lake breeze boundary and formed a quasi-linear convective system that advanced toward the Genesee Valley. Multiple reports of wind damage were received, and a NWS storm survey confirmed a tornado in the Town of Alexander in Genesee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were down at Thum Road and Bayview Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season nor'easter tracked up the East Coast from April 18-19, 2022 resulting in widespread precipitation across the region. Precipitation started in the form of rain, but mixed with and changed over to snow as colder air built into the region. The heaviest snow fell across the higher elevations (i.e., Adirondacks, western Mohawk Valley and eastern Catskills) where snowfall amounts were mainly in the 7 to 14 inch range with a few localized amounts up to 18 inches. Valley areas received less snow as rain mixed in longer with amounts ranging anywhere from less than an inch to up to 6 inches. In addition to the snow, strong winds accompanied this event with widespread gusts ranging from 30 to 50 mph.||The combination of heavy snow and strong winds resulted in widespread power outages across a span of central New York state as it brought down countless trees, limbs and power lines. This included portions of the eastern Catskills, Mohawk Valley, Adirondacks and Lake George/Saratoga region. Over 50 percent of the customers tracked in Herkimer and Hamilton counties were without power at some point. A few vehicle crashes were also reported across the area.||A state of emergency was issued for Herkimer and Hamilton counties so residents can limit travel so crews could clean up roadways. A snow emergency went into effect for the Village of Herkimer. New York Governor Kathy Hochul activated the State Emergency Operations Center in Albany where staff from multiple Stage Agencies and the Office of Emergency Management worked together to coordinate requests for assistance from local governments.||The event also prompted Herkimer College to close on Tuesday.||Key impacts: tree and power line damage, road closures, travel delays, power outages, vehicle accidents, school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition, winds occasionally gusted up to 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the area brought multiple rounds of rain on April 7th. The front approached and a low pressure system developed over the area resulting in moderate to heavy rain over the Lower Hudson Valley in New York. 2 to 3 inches of rain fell over a several hour period. This resulted in river and poor drainage flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water Rescue of occupant in vehicle stuck in flood waters on Grotke Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Central New York, triggering multiple thunderstorms. An unstable environment supported many severe thunderstorms. While damaging wind gusts were the main hazard, large hail was also reported. Most of the damage was from trees and wires being knocked down. However, there was a surveyed microburst in Cayuga County near Venice Center that resulted in multiple farm buildings that had parts or all of their roof ripped off, a small farm building being knocked off its foundation by a large tree, and multiple large trees in the area snapped or knocked over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees.

A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over southeastern New York that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NY Department of Highways reported a downed tree in Port Washington, NY on Route 101 northbound between Ohio Avenue and Delaware Avenue at 508 AM Local Standard Time with one lane closed.

A late-season winter storm impacted Central New York in mid-April. Snow-to-liquid ratios were low resulting in heavy snowfall. Snowfall totals exceeded a foot in some locations.  The heavy snow brought down trees and powerlines resulting in widespread power outages. The entries approximate the number of customers without power using pweroutage.us with data being valid in the morning of the 19th. At one point, over 100,000 customers in the state of New York were without power. The full extent of damage from the storm is unknown but damage values were estimated on the approximate number of customers without power in each county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 12 inches. The heavy snow caused trees and powers lines to fall, resulting in a number of customers without power. Since data is not divided by NWS zones, the approximate number of customers is covered in the Southern Oneida entry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the upper levels a negatively tilted trough approached from the west as associated surface low pressure formed and deepened as it passed to the north and west of the CWA. A strong cold front extending down from the surface low moved through the area allowing for severe thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires down on North Street in Middletown 2 miles NW of Unionville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Central New York, triggering multiple thunderstorms. An unstable environment supported many severe thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 90 mph microburst was determined to have hit Roach's Farm in|Venice, NY. There were four barns that sustained roof damage with|metal panels being lofted. The furthest debris was about 200 |yards from the barn. Some of the metal panels had been lofted into|the power lines along Stewart's Corners Rd but had been cleaned |prior to our survey. There were several pieces of metal roofing |that were still located in trees at the time of our survey. At the|intersection of Ford Rd and Stewart's Corners Rd, there was a |Davis weather station that was sheltered from the southwest, but |recorded a measured 59 mph gust during the storm. ||The strongest damage indicators were on the northwestern edge of |the damage path along Ford Rd, where several trees were snapped. |Three of the hemlock trees lining Ford road were snapped roughly |10 to 15 feet off the ground. Behind those trees, there were a |group of maple trees with several of those being snapped in |addition to the hemlocks. One of the maple trees fell onto a farm |building and shifted the entire building off the wooden foundation|to the east northeast. The building likely shifted due to the |tree falling, however the wooden polls on the northwestern edge of|the building had been lifted out of the ground by about 4 inches.|Other damage was also seen around the area, generally to the east|where another farm building lost metal roofing and a large |hardwood tree had its trunk snapped, however they were not along |the path of the stronger winds.

Precipitation started in the form of rain, but mixed with and changed over to snow as colder air built into the region. The heaviest snow fell across the higher elevations (i.e., Adirondacks, western Mohawk Valley and eastern Catskills) where snowfall amounts were mainly in the 7 to 14 inch range with a few localized amounts up to 18 inches. Valley areas received less snow as rain mixed in longer with amounts ranging anywhere from less than an inch to up to 6 inches. In addition to the snow, strong winds accompanied this event with widespread gusts ranging from 30 to 50 mph.||The combination of heavy snow and strong winds resulted in widespread power outages across a span of central New York state as it brought down countless trees, limbs and power lines. This included portions of the eastern Catskills, Mohawk Valley, Adirondacks and Lake George/Saratoga region. Over 50 percent of the customers tracked in Herkimer and Hamilton counties were without power at some point. A few vehicle crashes were also reported across the area.||A state of emergency was issued for Herkimer and Hamilton counties so residents can limit travel so crews could clean up roadways. A snow emergency went into effect for the Village of Herkimer. New York Governor Kathy Hochul activated the State Emergency Operations Center in Albany where staff from multiple Stage Agencies and the Office of Emergency Management worked together to coordinate requests for assistance from local governments.||The event also prompted Herkimer College to close on Tuesday.||Key impacts: tree and power line damage, road closures, travel delays, power outages, vehicle accidents, school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition, winds occasionally gusted up to 25 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system centered near the eastern Great Lakes Region continued to strengthen as it moved northeast on December 23, 2022. This brought high winds over southeastern New York that morning as the pressure gradient tightened over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NY Department of Highways reported downed wires with two lanes blocked on Route 9W at Christian Herald Road in Upper Nyack, NY at 340 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Central New York, triggering multiple thunderstorms. An unstable environment supported many severe thunderstorms. While damaging wind gusts were the main hazard, large hail was also reported. Most of the damage was from trees and wires being knocked down. However, there was a surveyed microburst in Cayuga County near Venice Center that resulted in multiple farm buildings that had parts or all of their roof ripped off, a small farm building being knocked off its foundation by a large tree, and multiple large trees in the area snapped or knocked over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds pushed through eastern New York during the evening hours of March 7, 2022 resulting in numerous reports of downed trees and power lines. Behind the storms, high winds continued during the late evening of the 7th into the early morning hours of the 8th. Some gusts of 50 to 55 miles per hour were recorded, which resulted in additional downed trees and power lines. Around 13,000 customers lost power in the Albany area, 700 in the Mohawk Valley, 3,000 in the Glens Falls area, and 21,000 in the Mid-Hudson Valley and Catskills. Two schools in the Catskills were closed the following day due to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was reported throughout Greene County. Reports of damage started coming in between 830-930pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system resulted in a total of 5-10 inches across eastern New York on March 12, 2022. This resulted in speed reductions, road closures, and several car accidents. Accidents were common along area interstates including I-87 and I-90. Speed restrictions were in place along the NYS Thruway, reducing the speed limits to 45 mph.||Key Impacts: Travel Restrictions, car accidents, road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds pushed through eastern New York during the evening hours of March 7, 2022 resulting in numerous reports of downed trees and power lines. Behind the storms, high winds continued during the late evening of the 7th into the early morning hours of the 8th. Some gusts of 50 to 55 miles per hour were recorded, which resulted in additional downed trees and power lines. Around 13,000 customers lost power in the Albany area, 700 in the Mohawk Valley, 3,000 in the Glens Falls area, and 21,000 in the Mid-Hudson Valley and Catskills. Two schools in the Catskills were closed the following day due to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree was reported on Sharptown Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak low pressure areas traversed along a cold front that moved across northern NY during the early morning hours of March 12th. Rain/snow mixed developed in the early morning hours changing to snow before sunrise and continuing until early afternoon. Snowfall amounts ranged from several inches along the St. Lawrence Valley and Canadian border to 6 to 12 inches in the higher terrain of the eastern Adirondacks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front which behaved like an anafront with the upper level flow more or less parallel to the front produced snow on the backside of the precipitation shield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An advisory level snowfall of 2 to 4 inches fell mainly across western and northern portions of Orange County as rain changed to snow. Reports included 3.9 inches in Goshen from the public, 3.8 inches in Warwick from a trained spotter, 3.5 inches from a trained spotter in Gardnertown, and 2.0 inches from a trained spotter in Chester. Orange County airport (MGJ) reported one quarter mile visibility in snow from 10:05 am local time to 12:15 pm local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brought storms to the area. Most of the severe activity was just south of the NY/PA border. Prior to storms weakening, one cell did have strong wind gusts that knocked over a tree in Delaware County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on wires on Evans Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough moved through the area as associated low pressure passed to our north and west. The low pressure system dragged a cold front through our area, which sparked and maintained a line of severe thunderstorms to southeastern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large tree branches down along Bull Mill road in Chester.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and wires reported down on Park avenue in Tarrytown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak low pressure areas traversed along a cold front that moved across northern NY during the early morning hours of March 12th. Rain/snow mixed developed in the early morning hours changing to snow before sunrise and continuing until early afternoon. Snowfall amounts ranged from several inches along the St. Lawrence Valley and Canadian border to 6 to 12 inches in the higher terrain of the eastern Adirondacks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers with some embedded thunderstorms resulted in isolated flooding in Montgomery County in eastern New York on March 19, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montgomery County Sheriff reported minor flooding on roadways due to nearby creeks overflowing their banks. This included Millers Corners Road near Mill Point and Logtown Road near the town of Glen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds pushed through eastern New York during the evening hours of March 7, 2022 resulting in numerous reports of downed trees and power lines. Some gusts of 50 to 55 miles per hour were recorded, which resulted in additional downed trees and power lines. Around 13,000 customers lost power in the Albany area, 700 in the Mohawk Valley, 3,000 in the Glens Falls area, and 21,000 in the Mid-Hudson Valley and Catskills. Two schools in the Catskills were closed the following day due to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect bands developed over Onondaga and Madison Counties on the 27th and continued into the morning hours of the 28th. These bands extended far inland with the help of the multi-lake connection of the Georgian Bay and Lake Ontario. The bands re-intensified during early morning hours on the 28th. Totals generally ranged from just a few inches to nearly a foot. Lesser amounts were also reported on the edges of the heaviest bands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwest flow aloft with an mid level shortwave interacted with a cold front with low pressure offshore. This resulted in 3 to 5 inches of snow across western and northwestern portions of Orange County. No impacts were reported through Local Storm Reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Middletown received 5.0 inches via Fire Dept/Rescue and the public reported 4.8 inches in Pine Bush. A trained spotter reported 4.0 inches in Gardnertown. Trained spotters also reported 3.8 inches in Warwick, 3.4 inches in Chester, and 3.0 inches at 4 SWW Middletown, and 3.0 inches at 1 WNW Monroe.

Two schools in the Catskills were closed the following day due to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down near the intersection of Blenheim Hill Rd and North Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front over the area on August 23rd. These thunderstorms were slow moving and produced heavy rainfall over Long Island. The thunderstorms developed in an environment with 1.5 to 1.7 precipitable water and CAPE values of 1500 - 2000 J/kg. The thunderstorms produced locally up to 2 to 3 inches of rain in a 2 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closure near Deer Park Ave and Jericho Turnpike in Elwood due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video shows road flooding with stranded vehicle on Main Street in Northport NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clouds held down instability for most of the area during the day, but there was enough instability along New York/Pennsylvania state line to support a few strong to severe thunderstorms due to strong diurnal heating combined with precipitable water values generally above two inches. A weakly sheared environment was the main limiting factor that kept storms from becoming better organized over this area. One of these storms with a very elevated core briefly crossed the border of Allegany County before collapsing and producing wind damage near Willing and Alma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sunshine over the Finger Lakes on the brought sufficient sunshine to bring ample instability to a very moist air mass that was in place. The atmosphere was not highly sheared, however. Thunderstorms first developed east of Lake Ontario with some degree of rotation involved. The second round of storms was a line of convection that rolled in from Lake Erie during the early evening. Winds aloft were around 40 knots and with a linear structure, this resulted in localized gusts in the Lake Erie marine layer as well as some trees down in the Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two hundred year old tree was downed and crushed a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms formed during the afternoon. An interesting aspect was that the storm motion has storms moving from east to west due to the counterclockwise flow around an upper low over the Middle Atlantic States. A few clusters of storms increased in intensity and produced isolated damaging wind gusts with some trees down, especially across Ontario County. Torrential rain also occurred with the slow-moving storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the area forced thunderstorm development late in the day on August 9th. These slow moving thunderstorms developed in an area of 2 to 2.2 inches of precipitable water with CAPE values near 2000 J/kg. These thunderstorms dropped a quick 1 to 1.5 inches of rainfall in less than an hour, producing localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicle stuck in high water with two occupants trapped on Park Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow advected warm, humid air into the region creating an unstable environment in the afternoon. A disturbance moved across Central New York, triggering multiple thunderstorms. Some of these thunderstorms became severe with damaging wind gusts. Some property damage occurred to houses and vehicles and one person was injured after a tree and utility wires fell onto their car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of hot and humid weather occurred across eastern New York during the second half of July. Both the Albany and Poughkeepsie climate sites had a stretch of six consecutive days with high temperatures of 90 degrees Fahrenheit or above spanning July 19 to July 24. The addition of humidity led to heat indices peaking between 95 and 103 degrees during the afternoon hours across the Hudson Valley on July 20, 21, 23 and 24. The heat prompted the opening of cooling centers throughout the region to assist those that needed relief.

Severe thunderstorms developed within a hot and humid air mass across portions of eastern New York, especially for areas south of Interstate 90, during the afternoon and evening hours on July 24, 2022. Widespread wind damage occurred across these areas.||More than 20,000 customers lost power across Ulster and Dutchess counties including in Esopus, Rosendale, Kingston, Saugerties, Wawarsing, Hyde Park, Pleasant Valley and Poughkeepsie. Sporadic pockets of outages also occurred across the Capital Region and into portions of Rensselaer and Columbia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed near Route 9W and Black Creek Road in the Town of Esopus.

Widespread wind damage occurred across these areas.||More than 20,000 customers lost power across Ulster and Dutchess counties including in Esopus, Rosendale, Kingston, Saugerties, Wawarsing, Hyde Park, Pleasant Valley and Poughkeepsie. Sporadic pockets of outages also occurred across the Capital Region and into portions of Rensselaer and Columbia counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of trees and wires down in the hamlet of Tillson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were downed near Pine Plains and Woodland Drive in the Town of New Paltz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of convection moved through the region during the afternoon and first half of the ensuing night as a slow moving cold front approached from southern Ontario. The event was long lived due to high instability and shear. The first round of convection broke out along a lake breeze boundary that stretched from the Finger Lakes region back across the Southern Tier.  The next round of storms that developed after sunset produced wind damage and also very heavy rain. This was a rare event in that back to back severe thunderstorm watches were issued across the NWS Buffalo county warning area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees downed on telephone lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of hot and humid weather occurred across eastern New York during the second half of July. Both the Albany and Poughkeepsie climate sites had a stretch of six consecutive days with high temperatures of 90 degrees Fahrenheit or above spanning July 19 to July 24. The addition of humidity led to heat indices peaking between 95 and 103 degrees during the afternoon hours across the Hudson Valley on July 20, 21, 23 and 24. The heat prompted the opening of cooling centers throughout the region to assist those that needed relief.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees were reported near Piseco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm resulted in multiple severe wind damage reports over Ulster and Dutchess Counties during the evening of Wednesday July 13, 2022. Additionally, the supercell resulted in a tornado and macroburst in Ulster County near Kingston, NY. The Mayor announced a Local State of Emergency (restricting all un-necessary travel in and around Kingston) after thunderstorms produced extensive tree damage in the area. At one point, over 6,000 residents were without power in the city of Kingston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albany NWS Survey Team confirmed an EF-1 tornado touched down near Hurley Avenue. The tornado touched down near Hurley Avenue (Route 29) between Davis Street and Hillside Drive. The tornado moved southeast to just west of the NY State Thruway crossing a subdivision where it uprooted and snapped trees, displaced a car canopy and removed some shingles from a house. Many homes were damaged by fallen trees. Aerial photos showed a swath of downed trees from a second vortex which |moved along the northeast edge of the subdivision.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was video evidence of a waterspout on the Hudson River on July 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout was sighted on the Hudson River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst affected Galusha Island and nearby Fort Miller. Winds moved southeastward from the Hudson River across the island and then across U.S. Route 4 before continuing across Blodgett Road. Estimated maximum wind speed was 85 mph with a path length of 1.0 miles and width of 1.5 miles.||Damage was mostly concentrated near the Hudson River on Galusha Island where many large hard and softwood trees were uprooted and a few trees and large branches were snapped. Many trees fell onto homes and at least one tree fell on a car. One tree snapped and fell onto a home along North River Road in which a resident was pinned by the roof debris inside the partially collapsed area of the residence. In Fort Miller, two wooden light poles and at least two power poles were snapped. A few trees were down along Blodgett Road including one on a home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm strengthened just northwest of Skaneateles at |around 530 PM. The storm generated a hail core with 65 dBZ around |20k feet and 50 dBZ around 25k feet around 540 PM. Dry air |entrainment in the mid levels and steep low level lapse rates |allowed the core of the storm to accelerate towards the ground and|led to a significant microburst just NW of Marcellus between 545 |PM and 550 PM.||Downed trees and corn stalks on the north side of the damage area|were largely orientated in a NNE direction and on the south side |of the damage in a SSE direction indicating divergent flow at the |surface. Large healthy trees near the center of the microburst |were snapped near the base or half way up along with damage to |siding and gutters on a couple houses indicating winds near 90 |mph. A barn on the corner of Stump Rd and Townline Rd had some |metal roof sheeting torn off and displaced a couple hundred yards |from the origin. A structure was destroyed by a fallen tree near |the eastern edge of the microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system with a warm front pushed through Central New York, providing support for isolated storms. While instability was limited, there was enough to support one lone severe storm that knocked down a few trees in Steuben County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto the roadway at the intersection of County Route 98 and Saunders Road.

A supercell thunderstorm resulted in multiple severe wind damage reports over Ulster and Dutchess Counties during the evening of Wednesday July 13, 2022. Additionally, the supercell resulted in a tornado and macroburst in Ulster County near Kingston, NY. The Mayor announced a Local State of Emergency (restricting all un-necessary travel in and around Kingston) after thunderstorms produced extensive tree damage in the area. At one point, over 6,000 residents were without power in the city of Kingston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were reported down on Atwood Road.

Sporadic pockets of outages also occurred across the Capital Region and into portions of Rensselaer and Columbia counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of hot and humid weather occurred across eastern New York during the second half of July. Both the Albany and Poughkeepsie climate sites had a stretch of six consecutive days with high temperatures of 90 degrees Fahrenheit or above spanning July 19 to July 24. The addition of humidity led to heat indices peaking between 95 and 103 degrees during the afternoon hours across the Hudson Valley on July 20, 21, 23 and 24. The heat prompted the opening of cooling centers throughout the region to assist those that needed relief.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm resulted in multiple severe wind damage reports over Ulster and Dutchess Counties during the evening of Wednesday July 13, 2022. Additionally, the supercell resulted in a tornado and macroburst in Ulster County near Kingston, NY. The Mayor announced a Local State of Emergency (restricting all un-necessary travel in and around Kingston) after thunderstorms produced extensive tree damage in the area. At one point, over 6,000 residents were without power in the city of Kingston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Albany Survey Team confirmed a Macroburst that began about 9 miles west of Kingston and ended about 5 miles southwest of Kingston in Ulster County. Wide swaths of downed trees were noted along Lapla Road (and connecting roads) across the hill the road crosses. At least 2 trees fell on cars along Lapla Road. Wind damage consisting of broken, |snapped and uprooted trees continued southeast across U.S. Route 209. The Macroburst had a path length of 4 miles and width of 1750 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brought scattered thunderstorms to Central New York. The environment was unstable enough for a couple of thunderstorms to become severe. One thunderstorm produced a microburst with estimated winds of 90 mph. Most of the damage was trees and corn stalks knocked down, though there was also some structual damage to farm buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough was the main feature out of a few disturbances that triggered a line of thunderstorms that passed through Central New York. These storms had damaging wind gusts and knocked down numerous trees across the region. Some trees and debris landed on vehicles or structures. There were also a couple reports of fires started from downed wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Erie Canal Bike Trail between Amsterdam and Fort Hunter was reported as impassable due to many downed trees.
