EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Mohawk Drive near Fonda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The incipient lake effect bands formed over far southern Erie County early in the morning hours of Thursday, November 17. The much more intense portion of this event really started with a bang on Thursday evening as an incredibly strong dominant band of lake effect snow developed into the Buffalo area. This intense band continued through Friday evening and dropped multiple feet of snow that was focused on central Erie County as well as northern Jefferson County.||The band off of Lake Erie migrated north overnight Friday night into early Saturday, impacting downtown Buffalo, the Buffalo Airport, and the Northtowns with rates of up to six inches per hour. It then shifted even farther north into Niagara County during the daytime Saturday.||The band off of Lake Ontario shifted into far northern Jefferson County and the Saint Lawrence Valley Saturday and Saturday evening.||The bands off both lakes eventually again settled a bit farther south with impressive snow rates continuing. Multi-day accumulations of heavy snow crippled the Buffalo Southtowns and Watertown area. Selected totals from this lake effect event include 81.2 inches in Hamburg, 80 inches in Orchard Park, 29 inches in Little Valley and Cattaraugus, 27 inches in Fredonia, 48.6 inches in Warsaw, 27.3 inches in Mexico, 76 inches in Blasdell, 36.6 inches at the Buffalo Airport, 19.5 inches in Harrisville, 25.3 inches in Batavia, 19.5 inches in North Tonawanda, and 9.5 inches in Cleveland. New one and two day county records were set for snowfall in Genesee County, and a new two day snowfall record was set in Erie County.||During the storm, one 74 year old man died in Wyoming County due to exposure after exiting his car and getting lost due to whiteout conditions. Additionally, two Erie County men died due to overexertion while shoveling snow.

A strong cold front plowed across the lower Great Lakes. Widespread rain and gusty winds accompanied the front as colder air charged across Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. Rain changed to snow at higher elevations first east of Lake Erie, but it took several more hours for rain to change to snow at higher elevations east of Lake Ontario. The lake effect off Lake Erie increased quickly as a west-southwest wind favored a stronger more persistent band of lake effect snow across southern Erie County and Wyoming County. The arrival of an upper level disturbance and additional moisture further enhanced the lake effect. Snowfall rates peaked over 1 inch per hour and lightning strikes were observed during the height of the evening as snow bands moved southward east of both lakes. Considerable blowing and drifting was observed due to the dry nature of the snow and widespread wind gusts in excess of 30 mph. The residence time of the band as well as upslope enhancement favored the largest accumulations in the entire area over the southern Tug Hill east of Lake Ontario. Meanwhile, to the east of Lake Erie, the shifting nature of the bands as well as marginal temperatures in the lower elevations yielding snowfall totals below warning criteria.||Total snowfall during this event was very elevation dependent. Shoreline locations ultimately saw little if any snow accumulation, while over the higher terrain, 15 inches of snow was observed near West Leyden and 9 inches near Lewis in Lewis County.

Following the passing of a front, cold temperatures and flow off of Lake Ontario supported lake effect snow showers over Northern Oneida County. Snow began on November 30th and continued into December 1st, with most of it occurring after midnight on the 1st. The highest totals along the northern portion of the county border where nearly a foot of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 4 and 11 inches.

A cold front swept across Central New York after midnight on the 12th. This front helped push out the rain showers from remnants of Tropical System Nicole. However, a line of storms and gusty winds were observed along the front edge of this front. A few reports of downed trees and power lines occurred in Broome and Chenango Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow developed downwind of Lake Ontario and westerly flow kept the heaviest snowfall north of the NYS Thruway. The heaviest snowfall was reported in Northern Oneida County, where reports exceeded a foot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 7 and 14.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second significant lake effect snow event within a week impacted Oneida County. Heavy snowfall was observed along and north of the New York Thruway Corridor. Further south of this area, totals quickly dropped off to just a couple of inches. The highest reports were in the far northern portion of the county where nearly two feet of snowfall was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 8 and 21.5 inches.

The United States Drought Monitor (USDM) continued to indicate a small area of severe drought across far southeastern Columbia County in New York for much of September.||Precipitation the previous 30 days were about 50 to 75 percent of normal but below normal precipitation signals had persisted since the start of June. Above normal temperatures combining with below normal precipitation contributed to the rapid deterioration of drought conditions. Soaking rainfall events during the month of September gradually improved drought conditions with severe drought ending on September 27.||The New York State Drought Management Task Force continued a Drought Watch for the region until its removal from severe drought at the end of the month.

The United States Drought Monitor (USDM) continued to indicate severe drought conditions across southern and eastern portions of Dutchess County in New York through the month of September. ||Precipitation the previous 30 days were about 50 to 75 percent of normal but below normal precipitation signals had persisted since the start of June. Above normal temperatures combining with below normal precipitation contributed to the rapid deterioration of drought conditions. Soaking rainfall events during the month of September began to slowly improve drought conditions.||The New York State Drought Management Task Force continued to place the region in a Drought Watch. Homeowners were encouraged to voluntarily reduce outdoor water use.||The City of Beacon also continued a Stage 1 drought emergency which included water use restrictions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave trough approached aloft and severe thunderstorms were produced in the warm sector over southeastern NY along a pre-frontal trough ahead of a cold frontal passage. These storms produced damaging wind gusts and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team, in |coordination with Suffolk County Fire Rescue and Emergency |Services has confirmed an EF0 tornado in the Mattituck area of|Suffolk County NY.||Based on analysis of the storm survey findings and OKX dual pol |radar reflectivity, velocity and derived data (including a |tornadic debris signature), it was determined that an EF0 tornado|first touched down at Juniper Hill farms around 1117 pm, damaging|a greenhouse and downing a couple of large trees on the property.||The tornadic circulation likely lifted or skipped along as it|tracked east northeast across a one mile stretch of remote wooded|area, before touching down and shearing several tree tops and|large limbs in the vicinity of Cottage Way, Walnut Place, and |Lipco Road, between Sound Avenue and Old Sound Avenue. Peripheral |straight line wind damage to a large tree was noted near Shirley |Road and Horton Avenue to the north of Sound Avenue as well.||The tornadic circulation likely skipped east northeast in the|vicinity of Old Sound Avenue/Main Street over Love Lane and|Wickham Avenue with spotty tree damage noted, before definitively|touching down once again between Wickham Ave and Mary's Road to |the north of Pike Street, with a large maple tree sheared a few |feet from its base, minor siding and roof damage, and leaf |splatter on the west and south sides of homes. This most |prominent damage path, about 50 to 75 yards in width, continued |across Mary's Road, where 3 evergreen trees were snapped at their |base in a convergent pattern (the 2 southernmost trees laying in a|northeast direction and the northernmost tree laying in a |southeast direction). The tornado continued eastward across the |Mattituck-Cutchogue Jr/Sr high school track, twisting and damaging|a large scoreboard, overturning metals benches, and laying down a|100 ft section of wind fence on the north side of the adjacent |tennis courts in a northerly direction (again consistent with a |convergent wind pattern). The tornado then continued east over the|high school, ripping up large sections of asphalt roofing |material over one of the main buildings. The damage across the |high school grounds was the most severe along the path, consistent|with tornadic winds of 75 to 85 mph.||The tornado then exited east over an area of homes on Village |Lane, causing significant tree and fence damage to several homes, |before likely lifting around 1120 pm as the rotating storm |continued eastward over the farm fields and vineyards that lay to|the east. No visible damage was noted to the east of this point, |marking the presumed end of the tornado path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, non-thunderstorm gradient winds associated with a sharp cold front resulted in multiple downed tree reports and power outages across parts of eastern New York Wednesday afternoon into Thursday (November 30-December 1). Behind the cold front, blustery west winds off Lake Ontario resulted in lake effect snow beginning later in the evening on November and continuing on December 1. There were reports of lake effect snows accumulating more than 7 inches which verified our winter storm warnings for Herkimer and Hamilton Counties on December 1. Power outages were reported in Washington, Warren and Herkimer Counties.||Key Impacts: Power outages, Closed Roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 196 in the town of Kingsbury was closed between Farm Way and New Swamp Road due to a downed tree and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of a strong cold front, bands of lake effect snow developed off Lake Ontario impacting portions of the Adirondacks and Mohawk Valley and also extending into the Hudson Valley. This resulted in multiple car accidents across the Hudson Valley with one occurring Sunday morning and the others Sunday evening. Additionally, the band of lake effect snow over Herkimer County resulted in several inches of accumulation over parts of the county. In addition to the snows, it was also blustery with winds gusting between 25-45 mph over the area. ||Key Impacts - Car accidents.

A lightning strike sparked a wildfire at the Minnewaska State Park Preserve on August 27, 2022. More than 200 emergency responders, including New York members of the Army and Air National Guards and several state and local agencies, including firefighters from Quebec, and volunteers, assisted in the blaze. Dry weather led to a continued spread of the fire. The fire continued to burn into the beginning of September before finally being contained on September 4. The fire burned 142 acres in total and did not damage any structures as it remained over forestry areas.||The fire caused the park to close for all visitors through the Labor Day weekend.
Moderate to heavy rain with a few embedded thunderstorms resulted in a flooded roadway in Ulster County on September 5, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northbound US 9W Ramp to NY Route 199 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable synoptic pattern with sub-freezing, uniform southwest flow over the long fetch of Lake Ontario on Friday 11/18 created a epic lake effect snow band that moved across the eastern shores of Lake Ontario and eventually into southern St. Lawrence and Franklin counties in NY. Total snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches across southern Franklin county and 8 to 30+ inches in southern St. Lawrence county with snowfall rates in excess of 2-3 inches per hour at times in the heaviest snowfall communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals in SE St. Lawrence ranged from 12 to 35 inches with 35 inches in Star Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable synoptic pattern with sub-freezing, uniform southwest flow over the long fetch of Lake Ontario on Friday 11/18 created a epic lake effect snow band that moved across the eastern shores of Lake Ontario and eventually into southern St. Lawrence and Franklin counties in NY. Total snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches across southern Franklin county and 8 to 30+ inches in southern St. Lawrence county with snowfall rates in excess of 2-3 inches per hour at times in the heaviest snowfall communities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals in SW St. Lawrence ranged from 8 to 33 inches with Gouverneur reporting 33 inches, 16 inches in Fowler, 10 inches in Morristown and 8 inches in Ogdensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A batch of moderate rainfall with embedded heavy thunderstorms moved through the area on September 13th bringing flash flooding to the New York City metro area. These thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front with a low pressure developing overhead in an extremely moisture environment that had precipitable water values of 1.8-2. These thunderstorms dropped between 1 and 3 inches of rainfall in a several hour period resulting in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Hutchinson River Parkway northbound at Exit 9 in New Rochelle. All lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist air created an unstable environment in the afternoon across Central New York. A sweeping cold front moved through the region during the evening, kicking off thunderstorms. There was enough shear present for a storm in Steuben County to develop a weak, short-lived tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Binghamton NY confirmed with a storm|survey, that a tornado descended upon the Wayland area of Steuben County |New York from 733 to 738 PM Monday evening, September 19, 2002. During |the survey, convergent tree damage indicative of a tornado was found |starting around Buffalo Street just to the west-northwest of Wayland. |This included a few trunk snaps, several uprooted trees, and numerous |downed large branches. The defined narrow path of mostly tree damage |continued to the east-southeast, including through the south side of Wayland |itself, where winds are estimated to have reached to 90 mph before the |tornado dissipated about a half mile southeast of the center of Wayland. |This included a vehicle impaled by a large branch, an eyewitness account |of another vehicle being moved several inches sideways on the roadway, and |clean trunk snaps of two large softwood trees. Some roof damage occurred to |a few residences due to being struck by trees or large branches blown around |by the tornado. Other damage included collapsed fences and a crushed |backyard swimming pool.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved into the Central New York during the early morning hours on the 22nd. Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the cold front. There was enough instability to support a severe thunderstorm that had strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down power lines, resulting in a power outage on Seneca Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front and trailing cold front swept across Central New York. One storm developed in the warm sector and dropped quarter sized hail in Broome County. This storm then weakened but became stronger again in Delaware County as it approached the warm front. The shear from the warm front was enough for a weak tornado to develop.

A warm front and trailing cold front swept across Central New York. One storm developed in the warm sector and dropped quarter sized hail in Broome County. This storm then weakened but became stronger again in Delaware County as it approached the warm front. The shear from the warm front was enough for a weak tornado to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Binghamton NY confirmed with a |storm survey, that a tornado moved through the Walton area on the |evening of September 25th between 6:38 PM to 6:41 PM. During the |survey, tornado damage was first noted along Walton Mountain Rd |where trees fell in a convergent pattern. Talking to several |residents, winds came from multiple directions, with lawn furniture|and part of a deck railing blown northwest while trees in |the back yard were blown down facing southwest. Sporadic tree |damage was followed down to route 10 to an autoshop with some |metal sheeting ripped off of the roof and thrown about a 100 |yards. From there the damage picked back up across the West Branch|of the Delaware River along S River Rd. Sporadic tree damage |continued along the south shore of the river to just south of S |river Rd with the worst tree damage occurring near the end of the |tornado's track. Up until this section, the damage from the |tornado was EF0. Many large trees were snapped or uprooted and |fell in a cross stitch pattern for a section of forest that was |about 300 yards long and 50 yards wide where the EF1 damage was |found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid weather ahead of an approaching cold front led to heat indices reaching between 95F and 100F degrees as well as a few severe thunderstorms across eastern New York on August 30, 2022. Heat indices in excess of 95F degrees were concentrated along the Hudson River Valley. Severe thunderstorms were focused across the Upper Hudson Valley as well as southern Herkimer County. Damage was mainly due to downed trees and wires, though lightning strikes caused two separate house fires. Heavy rainfall also led to some minor street flooding around Glens Falls. A few power outages occurred across the Upper Hudson Valley as a result of the storms.

Amid a hot and humid air mass, showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on August 4, 2022. Some thunderstorms became severe producing downed trees and power lines as well as heavy rainfall leading to flash flooding. Flash flooding was mainly confined to the Town of Bethlehem as training thunderstorms produced upwards of 4 inches of rain in just a couple of hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed on Hartman Road in the City of Albany.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on August 17, 2022 in association with a disturbance off the Northeast US coast. These storms tracked from northeast to southwest. Damage was mainly confined to the Capital Region where a severe thunderstorm downed some trees and power lines across the region. In addition, sub-severe wind gusts led to some flying debris at the New York State Food Festival which was being held in Albany. Event managers cancelled the remainder of the event following the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were downed on the lower end of the City of Rensselaer.

Albany and Poughkeepsie also set a new record for the hottest 8-day start to August on record.||The stretch of hot weather led to the opening of multiple cooling centers across the region.

The United States Drought Monitor (USDM) indicated a small area of severe drought across southeastern Dutchess County in New York starting on August 9, 2022. The previous 30 days had seen less than half of normal rainfall with below normal precipitation persisting since the start of June. A period of hot and dry weather, especially during the first several days of August, contributed to this rapid deterioration of drought conditions. The severe drought expanded further to include more of Dutchess County starting on August 16. Severe drought in these areas continued into the month of September.||The New York State Drought Management Task Force placed the region in a Drought Watch beginning on August 11. Homeowners were encouraged to voluntarily reduce outdoor water use.||The City of Beacon also instituted a Stage 1 drought emergency which includes water use restrictions.

Heat indices in excess of 95F degrees were concentrated along the Hudson River Valley. Severe thunderstorms were focused across the Upper Hudson Valley as well as southern Herkimer County. Damage was mainly due to downed trees and wires, though lightning strikes caused two separate house fires. Heavy rainfall also led to some minor street flooding around Glens Falls. A few power outages occurred across the Upper Hudson Valley as a result of the storms.

A strong low pressure system passed to our north and west over the Great Lakes dragging a strong cold front through southeast NY. The front sparked severe thunderstorms that produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site XFSH in Fishers Island NY measured a 50 knot wind gust.

The United States Drought Monitor (USDM) indicated a small area of severe drought across far southeastern Columbia County in New York starting on August 16, 2022. The previous 30 days had seen less than half of normal rainfall with below normal precipitation persisting since the start of June. A period of hot and dry weather, especially during the first several days of August, contributed to this rapid deterioration of drought conditions. Severe drought in this area continued into the month of September.||The New York State Drought Management Task Force continued a Drought Watch for the region. Homeowners were encouraged to voluntarily reduce outdoor water use.

A lightning strike sparked a wildfire at the Minnewaska State Park Preserve on August 27, 2022. More than 200 emergency responders, including New York members of the Army and Air National Guards and several state and local agencies, including firefighters from Quebec, and volunteers, assisted in the blaze. Dry weather led to a continued spread of the fire. Much needed rainfall fell on August 30, but did not fully extinguish the fire. The fire continued to burn into the beginning of September before finally being contained. The fire burned 142 acres in total and did not damage any structures as it remained over forestry areas.||The fire caused the park to close for all visitors through the Labor Day weekend.

A tornado touched down in Wyoming County at approximately 10:40AM|EDT in the town of Java just southwest of the intersection of NY Route|78 and Chaffee Rd. The tornado then moved along an east-northeast|path near NY Route 78 for approximately 10 miles before|dissipating in the western portion of the Town of Gainesville just |west of NY Route 19 at about 10:55 AM EDT. The tornado was on the |ground for approximately 15 minutes.||The tornado reached a maximum intensity of EF-2, with winds of 115|mph. The maximum path width was about 500 yards. The majority of the|EF-2 rated damage was along the first two miles of the tornado path.|This was also the portion of the track with the most consistent and|continuous damage. In this portion of the track, the tornado did |significant damage to a barn. The older portion of the barn saw its|concrete block foundation toppled and the structure itself moved |about 25 feet. The newer portion of the barn was entirely destroyed. |Multiple hard and softwood trees were uprooted or saw their trunks|sheared off as the tornado tracked along NY Route 78 until it got to|about Pleasant Valley Road. ||Damage became all EF-1 or lower rated to the east of Pleasant Valley |Road through Hermitage. This included a few uprooted softwood trees|and some minor wall damage to a structure on Shaw Road. ||As the tornado crossed from the Town of Wethersfield and into the|western portion of the Town of Gainesville, damage became much less|continuous and likely indicative of a discontinuous path of the |tornado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Wyoming County at approximately 10:40AM EDT in the town of Java just southwest of the intersection of NY Route 78 and Chaffee Rd. The tornado then moved along an east-northeast path near NY Route 78 for approximately 10 miles before dissipating in the western portion of the Town of Gainesville just west of NY Route 19 at about 10:55 AM EDT. The tornado was on the ground for approximately 15 minutes.||The tornado reached a maximum intensity of EF-2, with winds of 115 mph. The maximum path width was about 500 yards. The majority of the EF-2 rated damage was along the first two miles of the tornado path. This was also the portion of the track with the most consistent and continuous damage. In this portion of the track, the tornado did significant damage to a barn. The older portion of the barn saw its concrete block foundation toppled and the structure itself moved about 25 feet. The newer portion of the barn was entirely destroyed. Multiple hard and softwood trees were uprooted or saw their trunks sheared off as the tornado tracked along NY Route 78 until it got to|about Pleasant Valley Road. ||Damage became all EF-1 or lower rated to the east of Pleasant Valley Road through Hermitage. This included a few uprooted softwood trees and some minor wall damage to a structure on Shaw Road. ||As the tornado crossed from the Town of Wethersfield and into the western portion of the Town of Gainesville, damage became much less continuous and likely indicative of a discontinuous path of the tornado. All damage on this eastern extend of the path was minor tree damage and rated EF-0 before damage ceased about one mile west|of NY Route 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across a moderately unstable air mass across Ontario province into northwest NY during the afternoon and early evening of July 19th. Scattered thunderstorms moved from Ontario into St. Lawrence county NY with several reports of damaging winds in the form of downed trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees down across Route 11C in Winthrop.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down along Route 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed along a weak lake breeze boundary over the western Southern Tier during the peak heating of the day. One stronger thunderstorm produced several reports of wind damage in Cattaraugus county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed across Little Valley, Ellicottville and Franklinville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms along and ahead of an approaching cold front from the west resulted in a few damaging wind reports including a few downed trees and wires across eastern New York Friday night July 1, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees reported down in Fort Johnson. There were also cars and a house damaged due to fallen trees. Multiple trees were also reported down in St. Mary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward lifting warm front brought a hot and humid air mass to portions of eastern New York on July 28, 2022. Heat indices reached the mid-90s to lower 100s across the Hudson River Valley from the Capital District and points south. ||During the afternoon and early evening hours, showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front. A couple of these storms became severe producing isolated wind damage across portions of Rensselaer and Columbia counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought widespread accumulating snowfall across eastern New York on December 11, 2022 before tapering off during the early morning hours on December 12, 2022. Many areas picked up between 3 and 6 inches of snow. A band of more moderate snow set up across portions of the eastern Mohawk Valley, Capital District and the northern and central Taconics where snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches were common. Numerous accidents were reported as a result of the snow covered and slippery road conditions.||Key Impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, snow-covered roads.

A high-impact, long-duration winter storm affected the region from December 15-17, 2022. This was an elevation-dependent storm with heavy snowfall for the higher elevations and a mix of rain and snow in the valleys. Snowfall totals of 12-24 inches were common across areas above 1000 feet with mainly 6 inches or less below. A brief period of sleet and freezing rain occurred across portions of the Hudson Valley during the evening hours on December 15 before precipitation changed over to plain rain. Rain then changed over to snow during the daytime hours on December 16.||This was a heavy, wet snow and the weight of the snow caused downed trees and power lines resulting in scattered power outages. Hamilton County was hit hardest with power outages with over 30 percent of customers tracked without power at some point during the event. A few vehicle accidents also occurred as a result of the slippery road conditions. Delays and cancellations occurred at the Albany International Airport. A few snow emergencies and snow removal parking bans were in place across the region.||Key Impacts: Travel Delays, Tree Damage, Power Outages, Vehicle Accidents.

A cold front moved over portions of Central New York. On the west side, conditions remained rain-free and mostly clear throughout the morning, allowing for the environment to become unstable. A passing cold front later in the day brought showers and thunderstorms, with a few storms becoming severe. Severe storm indices also indicated the potential for large hail this day. Around half of the reports received were from damaging wind gusts while the other half were for severe and significant hail. Many hail reports ranged from around 2 all the way up to 3. While hail damage was unknown, one of the many local dealerships did host a sale for cars that were slightly damaged.

A low pressure system brought widespread accumulating snowfall across eastern New York on December 11, 2022 before tapering off during the early morning hours on December 12, 2022. Many areas picked up between 3 and 6 inches of snow. A band of more moderate snow set up across portions of the eastern Mohawk Valley, Capital District and the northern and central Taconics where snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches were common. Numerous accidents were reported as a result of the snow covered and slippery road conditions.||Key Impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, snow-covered roads.

A band of more moderate snow set up across portions of the eastern Mohawk Valley, Capital District and the northern and central Taconics where snowfall amounts of 6 to 9 inches were common. Numerous accidents were reported as a result of the snow covered and slippery road conditions.||Key Impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents, snow-covered roads.

A high-impact, long-duration winter storm affected the region from December 15-17, 2022. This was an elevation-dependent storm with heavy snowfall for the higher elevations and a mix of rain and snow in the valleys. Snowfall totals of 12-24 inches were common across areas above 1000 feet with mainly 6 inches or less below. A brief period of sleet and freezing rain occurred across portions of the Hudson Valley during the evening hours on December 15 before precipitation changed over to plain rain. Rain then changed over to snow during the daytime hours on December 16.||This was a heavy, wet snow and the weight of the snow caused downed trees and power lines resulting in scattered power outages. Hamilton County was hit hardest with power outages with over 30 percent of customers tracked without power at some point during the event. A few vehicle accidents also occurred as a result of the slippery road conditions. Delays and cancellations occurred at the Albany International Airport. A few snow emergencies and snow removal parking bans were in place across the region.||Key Impacts: Travel Delays, Tree Damage, Power Outages, Vehicle Accidents.

A cold front moved over portions of Central New York. On the west side, conditions remained rain-free and mostly clear throughout the morning, allowing for the environment to become unstable. A passing cold front later in the day brought showers and thunderstorms, with a few storms becoming severe. Severe storm indices also indicated the potential for large hail this day. Around half of the reports received were from damaging wind gusts while the other half were for severe and significant hail. Many hail reports ranged from around 2 all the way up to 3. While hail damage was unknown, one of the many local dealerships did host a sale for cars that were slightly damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was measured 30 minutes after it fell and likely was large when it fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of thunderstorms developed over southern Ontario ahead of an approaching weak cold front. While the initial waves of convection fizzled out as they approached the Niagara Peninsula, the final wave held together and crossed into the Niagara Frontier interacting with increasing shear and instability resulting in several reports of thunderstorm wind damage into the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed and blocking the road.

A cold front and mid-level disturbance moved across a moderately unstable air mass during the late night hours of July 24th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms accompanied the cold front with a few isolated severe storms that produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of trees and utility lines down in the Morley vicinity, including at the Intersection of Routes 68 and 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The bulk of the area was ensconced within a very warm and sultry air mass with dewpoints of 70-75 degrees. This allowed for a very unstable air mass that was also highly sheared. While a cold front was bearing down on the region from Ontario, most of the severe weather was initiated along a lake breeze that pushed east from Lake Erie. The thunderstorms that initiated developed very strong cores aloft and were responsible for several large hail reports of up to golf ball size. Additionally, the strongest storms of the day ripped through the Genesee Valley. Those storms were surveyed by a team from the NWS, and winds of 100 mph were estimated to be responsible for the damage found there.

Skies cleared as high pressure moved into the area behind one of the first strong cold fronts of the season. Temperatures fell off across the area with widespread frost in the Southern Tier and North Country. Freezing temperatures were also widespread in Lewis County. Selected low temperatures included 29 in Croghan, 30 in Highmarket, and 32 in Copenhagen.

A persistent band of lake-effect snow off Lake Ontario amid a bitter cold air mass dropped southward just enough to bring heavy snowfall to far northern portions of Herkimer County on December 25-26, 2022. Snowfall totals of around 10 inches were reported with this band before it lifted back northward out of the county.||Key impacts: none.

A frontal system pushed across the region during the afternoon and early evening hours on June 12, 2022, resulting in a few showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York. Locally heavy rainfall occurred across portions of southern Herkimer County which resulted in a flooded road in the Town of Frankfort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road flooding occurred in the Town of Frankfort at Center Road and Mucky Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts continued on December 1, 2022 in association with a frontal system that pushed across the region on November 30, 2022. Westerly wind gusts between 40-50 mph were observed across some areas which led to a few power outages. In addition, a band of lake-effect snow affected portions of Herkimer and Hamilton counties with up to a foot of accumulation. ||Key impacts: power outages.

Prior to the arrival of a prefrontal trough, some thunderstorms managed to take advantage of limited instability in Central New York in the afternoon of the 26th. One thunderstorm over Chenango County became severe and knocked trees and wires down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto the road at the intersection of Lyon Brook and Wahlberg Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCC (mesoscale convective complex) developed over west-central New York late in the day on the 15th. This system swept across Central New York after midnight on the 16th, triggering multiple storms. Some of the storms became severe with damaging wind gusts being the main hazard.

A short wave trough, out ahead of an approaching cold front, crossed the area during the afternoon and triggered showers and thunderstorms along the lake breeze boundaries. A thunderstorm that developed over the Niagara Peninsula slowly tracked southeast becoming better organized and expanding in size. This storm crossed over the Buffalo Metro, then it proceeded to the southeast. Wind damage was eventually reported from this storm in Cattaraugus County. Up until that point, weaker activity had crossed along the Lake Ontario shoreline, resulting in one wind report near Irondequoit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a pre-frontal trough in the Finger Lakes region and tracked northeast into north central New York. The eastern Lake Ontario region was mostly sunny through the day which allowed for untapped instability as storms were approaching the region. Even with weak shear, storms were able to become semi-organized across Oswego and western Lewis counties with one lone report of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed onto Osceola Road.

This system swept across Central New York after midnight on the 16th, triggering multiple storms. Some of the storms became severe with damaging wind gusts being the main hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires near Slater Road and Route 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After storms early in the morning, a cold front brought another round of showers and thunderstorms to Central New York during the day. A warm airmass helped create an unstable environment and supported severe thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down wires near Peterman Road and Ovid Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold and unstable air flowed southward into the region with the first push of Canadian air of the season into western New York. This resulted in a broad pool of mid-level cold air pushing over the relatively warm waters of the Great Lakes. Due to the shear profile, thunderstorms developed over the Great Lakes and resulted in isolated waterspouts. Further, during and subsequent to peak daytime heating, there was enough instability and shear to result in a few damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported downed onto a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After storms early in the morning, a cold front brought another round of showers and thunderstorms to Central New York during the day. A warm airmass helped create an unstable environment and supported severe thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a large tree in Sunset Campgrounds. The tree fell between two campsites, barely missing the two RVs parked on the campsites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front pushed into the St. Lawrence Valley and northern Adirondacks during the morning hours of June 23, 2022. Bands of showers and thunderstorms, some with moderate to locally heavy rainfall were observed in this region, especially in the vicinity of Malone, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms trained across portions of northeastern Franklin County, NY in the vicinity of Malone and the hamlet of Whippleville. One home in Whippleville was evacuated due to high water from a creek feeding into the nearby Salmon River. A driveway crossing the creek at the residence was overwhelmed and clogged by debris, impounding water, which quickly rose, flooding the residence. County Road 25, 2 miles south-southeast of Whippleville was also closed due to high water as a farm pond and connected drainage overflowed onto the highway. Additionally, a portion of Webster Road, approximately  1 mile southwest of Whippleville near the Malone Fish and Game Club was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lake Ontario lake breeze sagged southward early in the evening and intersected an enhanced cumulus field residing over eastern Erie, eastern Niagara, Genesee, Wyoming, and Livingston counties. At the same time, a subtle short wave trough aloft crossed over the aforementioned area. Capping had kept robust instability at bay through the afternoon until the combination of these features was finally able to allow convective initiation. The storms then busted through the cap and aided by 35-40 knots of shear, the storms grew rapidly and managed to maintain intensity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed and a utility pole was snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic lake effect blizzard occurred northeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario during the Christmas holiday weekend. The combination of high winds in excess of 70 mph and heavy lake effect snow resulted in devastating impacts across western New York and also east of Lake Ontario from December 23 through December 27. ||Warmer air initially was in place over the majority of the region with widespread middle and upper 30s being common in the pre-dawn hours on December 23. Bombogenesis occurred with an incoming surface low pressure center that crossed the Great Lakes and brought abruptly colder and vastly windier conditions into the area behind its adjoining cold front. Rain quickly transitioned to snow immediately behind the front, while temperatures and wind chills went into a free fall. This dramatic drop in temperature led to a flash freeze behind the front Friday morning, and by noon, temperatures across western New York were in the teens with wind chills already well below zero. ||Temperatures off the surface took a tumble Friday and yielding strong lake-induced instability over the fully open waters of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. This produced ideal lapse rates needed for lake effect processes. Meanwhile, a 240 degree flow over the lakes produced a long fetch, particularly over Lake Erie. Finally, unidirectional winds with speeds of 50-60 knots in a well-mixed boundary layer led to instantaneous blizzard conditions across the Niagara Frontier and Northtowns northeast of Lake Erie and across Jefferson County northeast of Lake Ontario. In fact, blizzard conditions started at the Buffalo Airport at 8:39 AM and at 1:44 PM at Watertown Airport on Friday, December 23. The strong winds led to wider and longer lake bands across the region. Due to the extreme conditions, snowflakes were shredded into fragments as they fell. This led to terrible visibility and impossible driving conditions. The highest wind gusts were recorded Friday morning northeast of Lake Erie and Friday afternoon northeast of Lake Ontario. Some of the highest wind gusts include 79 mph at the base of the Buffalo Skyway, 72 mph at the Buffalo Airport, 74 mph at Niagara Falls, 59 mph at Watertown Airport, and 54 mph at Fort Drum Airfield. The strength of these winds led to widespread power outages northeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario including the cities of Buffalo and Watertown. ||The lake bands shifted slightly to the north Friday night, which compounded the impacts across the Buffalo and Watertown Metro areas. Snow added up quickly northeast of the Lakes with totals of 1-2 feet across northern Erie, southern Niagara, and northern Jefferson counties by Saturday morning. A daily snowfall record of 22.3 inches at Buffalo Airport was recorded on December 23. Snowfall rates were very hard to quantify due to the strong winds. The lake band shifted slightly north across Niagara, Orleans, and northern Erie counties Saturday. The backing winds led to a shorter fetch across Lake Ontario, therefore the band weakened across northern Jefferson county Saturday afternoon. After 24.5 consecutive hours of blizzard conditions, the blizzard ended at the Watertown Airport at 2:25 PM, December 24. Meanwhile northeast of Lake Erie, southwest winds continued to blow 50 to 60 mph Saturday with continued blizzard conditions, even for locations just outside of the bands due to blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall totals closed in on 3 feet at the Buffalo Airport by Saturday evening. ||The blizzard ended at the Buffalo Airport at 9:54 PM on December 24. By Sunday morning, a shortwave trough moved across the eastern Great Lakes, which finally led to a notable wind shift. Winds further weakened as they veered westerly. This wind shift led to a greater fetch off of Lake Ontario, which allowed for lake effect snow to intensify across Jefferson County and eventually the Tug Hill. Meanwhile, east of Lake Erie, lake effect bands moved south and settled across the Southtowns by late Saturday night. Snowfall rates of two to three inches per hour were persistent east of Lake Erie Saturday night through Sunday. Snowfall rates of three to four inches per hour were estimated across the Tug Hill by Sunday. Snow totals surpassed 2 feet across Jefferson County by Sunday evening. The rising sun showed the impact of the historic blizzard across the Northtowns Sunday morning. Scenes included high drifts of snow, ongoing power outages, untouched roads, closed interstates, and hundreds of stranded cars. ||A deep trough persisted across the eastern United States with cold air still providing conditions ideal for lake effect processes across the eastern Great Lakes Sunday night into Monday. Boundary layer flow backed to the west-southwest across Lake Erie late Sunday night, and the lake band moved back north through the City of Buffalo with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour. The band continued to move north and weaken due to an increase in wind shear and shorter fetch. An intense lake band persisted east of Lake Ontario with three to four inch per hour rates across southern Jefferson and Lewis counties Sunday night into Monday. A wind shift to the southwest weakened and moved the lake band into northern Jefferson County by Monday afternoon. The last hurrah came Monday night when synoptic moisture reinvigorated the lake bands northeast of both Lakes. By Tuesday morning, the lake effect event ended as strong subsidence moved into the Great Lakes region. Storm total snowfall amounts exceeded 50 inches northeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. ||The historic blizzard and lake effect snow event spanned five days and included the Christmas holiday. This high impact event led to school and government closures on Friday, December 23, impossible travel, and widespread power outages across the Buffalo and Watertown Metro areas. The blizzard led to the loss of lives in Niagara and Erie counties. The halt to the transportation sector included closures on the New York State Thruway (I-90) from Rochester, NY, to the Pennsylvania state line and travel bans across Niagara and Erie counties that lasted 1-2 days after blizzard conditions ended. The Buffalo and Watertown Airports closed for multiple days during the holiday weekend.||Selected storm total snowfall reports from this five day event include 59.7 inches in North Tonawanda, 51.9 inches at the Buffalo Airport, 48.3 inches at Henderson Harbor, 47.2 inches in Watertown, 50.8 inches in Deferiet, 14.4 inches in Highmarket, 9 inches in Richland, 8.2 inches in Brockport, 19.3 inches in Warsaw, 16 inches in Batavia, 44.6 inches in Lake View, 37 inches in Orchard Park, 32.5 inches in Kenmore, and 9.6 inches in Mayville.||Additionally, with the extremely strong wind gusts, the fourth largest seiche on record on Lake Erie occurred. A notable seiche was evident on Lake Ontario, as well. Heavy freezing spray from the lake ensconced lakeshore homes with ice, and water ended up closing Route 5 in Hamburg. Lake Erie at Buffalo peaked at 10.7 feet and was in flood for 17 hours. The seiche on Lake Ontario likewise raised the water level 2 feet at Cape Vincent.

The lake band shifted slightly north across Niagara, Orleans, and northern Erie counties Saturday. The backing winds led to a shorter fetch across Lake Ontario, therefore the band weakened across northern Jefferson county Saturday afternoon. After 24.5 consecutive hours of blizzard conditions, the blizzard ended at the Watertown Airport at 2:25 PM, December 24. Meanwhile northeast of Lake Erie, southwest winds continued to blow 50 to 60 mph Saturday with continued blizzard conditions, even for locations just outside of the bands due to blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall totals closed in on 3 feet at the Buffalo Airport by Saturday evening. ||The blizzard ended at the Buffalo Airport at 9:54 PM on December 24. By Sunday morning, a shortwave trough moved across the eastern Great Lakes, which finally led to a notable wind shift. Winds further weakened as they veered westerly. This wind shift led to a greater fetch off of Lake Ontario, which allowed for lake effect snow to intensify across Jefferson County and eventually the Tug Hill. Meanwhile, east of Lake Erie, lake effect bands moved south and settled across the Southtowns by late Saturday night. Snowfall rates of two to three inches per hour were persistent east of Lake Erie Saturday night through Sunday. Snowfall rates of three to four inches per hour were estimated across the Tug Hill by Sunday. Snow totals surpassed 2 feet across Jefferson County by Sunday evening. The rising sun showed the impact of the historic blizzard across the Northtowns Sunday morning. Scenes included high drifts of snow, ongoing power outages, untouched roads, closed interstates, and hundreds of stranded cars. ||A deep trough persisted across the eastern United States with cold air still providing conditions ideal for lake effect processes across the eastern Great Lakes Sunday night into Monday. Boundary layer flow backed to the west-southwest across Lake Erie late Sunday night, and the lake band moved back north through the City of Buffalo with snowfall rates of one to two inches per hour. The band continued to move north and weaken due to an increase in wind shear and shorter fetch. An intense lake band persisted east of Lake Ontario with three to four inch per hour rates across southern Jefferson and Lewis counties Sunday night into Monday. A wind shift to the southwest weakened and moved the lake band into northern Jefferson County by Monday afternoon. The last hurrah came Monday night when synoptic moisture reinvigorated the lake bands northeast of both Lakes. By Tuesday morning, the lake effect event ended as strong subsidence moved into the Great Lakes region. Storm total snowfall amounts exceeded 50 inches northeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. ||The historic blizzard and lake effect snow event spanned five days and included the Christmas holiday. This high impact event led to school and government closures on Friday, December 23, impossible travel, and widespread power outages across the Buffalo and Watertown Metro areas. The blizzard led to the loss of lives in Niagara and Erie counties. The halt to the transportation sector included closures on the New York State Thruway (I-90) from Rochester, NY, to the Pennsylvania state line and travel bans across Niagara and Erie counties that lasted 1-2 days after blizzard conditions ended. The Buffalo and Watertown Airports closed for multiple days during the holiday weekend.||Selected storm total snowfall reports from this five day event include 59.7 inches in North Tonawanda, 51.9 inches at the Buffalo Airport, 48.3 inches at Henderson Harbor, 47.2 inches in Watertown, 50.8 inches in Deferiet, 14.4 inches in Highmarket, 9 inches in Richland, 8.2 inches in Brockport, 19.3 inches in Warsaw, 16 inches in Batavia, 44.6 inches in Lake View, 37 inches in Orchard Park, 32.5 inches in Kenmore, and 9.6 inches in Mayville.||Additionally, with the extremely strong wind gusts, the fourth largest seiche on record on Lake Erie occurred. A notable seiche was evident on Lake Ontario, as well. Heavy freezing spray from the lake ensconced lakeshore homes with ice, and water ended up closing Route 5 in Hamburg. Lake Erie at Buffalo peaked at 10.7 feet and was in flood for 17 hours. The seiche on Lake Ontario likewise raised the water level 2 feet at Cape Vincent.

A notable seiche was evident on Lake Ontario, as well. Heavy freezing spray from the lake ensconced lakeshore homes with ice, and water ended up closing Route 5 in Hamburg. Lake Erie at Buffalo peaked at 10.7 feet and was in flood for 17 hours. The seiche on Lake Ontario likewise raised the water level 2 feet at Cape Vincent.

A historic lake effect blizzard occurred northeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario during the Christmas holiday weekend. The combination of high winds in excess of 70 mph and heavy lake effect snow resulted in devastating impacts across western New York and also east of Lake Ontario from December 23 through December 27. ||Warmer air initially was in place over the majority of the region with widespread middle and upper 30s being common in the pre-dawn hours on December 23. Bombogenesis occurred with an incoming surface low pressure center that crossed the Great Lakes and brought abruptly colder and vastly windier conditions into the area behind its adjoining cold front. Rain quickly transitioned to snow immediately behind the front, while temperatures and wind chills went into a free fall. This dramatic drop in temperature led to a flash freeze behind the front Friday morning, and by noon, temperatures across western New York were in the teens with wind chills already well below zero. ||Temperatures off the surface took a tumble Friday and yielding strong lake-induced instability over the fully open waters of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. This produced ideal lapse rates needed for lake effect processes. Meanwhile, a 240 degree flow over the lakes produced a long fetch, particularly over Lake Erie. Finally, unidirectional winds with speeds of 50-60 knots in a well-mixed boundary layer led to instantaneous blizzard conditions across the Niagara Frontier and Northtowns northeast of Lake Erie and across Jefferson County northeast of Lake Ontario. In fact, blizzard conditions started at the Buffalo Airport at 8:39 AM and at 1:44 PM at Watertown Airport on Friday, December 23. The strong winds led to wider and longer lake bands across the region. Due to the extreme conditions, snowflakes were shredded into fragments as they fell. This led to terrible visibility and impossible driving conditions. The highest wind gusts were recorded Friday morning northeast of Lake Erie and Friday afternoon northeast of Lake Ontario. Some of the highest wind gusts include 79 mph at the base of the Buffalo Skyway, 72 mph at the Buffalo Airport, 74 mph at Niagara Falls, 59 mph at Watertown Airport, and 54 mph at Fort Drum Airfield. The strength of these winds led to widespread power outages northeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario including the cities of Buffalo and Watertown. ||The lake bands shifted slightly to the north Friday night, which compounded the impacts across the Buffalo and Watertown Metro areas. Snow added up quickly northeast of the Lakes with totals of 1-2 feet across northern Erie, southern Niagara, and northern Jefferson counties by Saturday morning. The lake band shifted slightly north across Niagara, Orleans, and northern Erie counties Saturday. The backing winds led to a shorter fetch across Lake Ontario, therefore the band weakened across northern Jefferson county Saturday afternoon. After 24.5 consecutive hours of blizzard conditions, the blizzard ended at the Watertown Airport at 2:25 PM, December 24. Meanwhile northeast of Lake Erie, southwest winds continued to blow 50 to 60 mph Saturday with continued blizzard conditions, even for locations just outside of the bands due to blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall totals closed in on 3 feet at the Buffalo Airport by Saturday evening. ||The blizzard ended at the Buffalo Airport at 9:54 PM on December 24. Meanwhile, east of Lake Erie, lake effect bands moved south and settled across the Southtowns by late Saturday night. Snowfall rates of two to three inches per hour were persistent east of Lake Erie Saturday night through Sunday. Snowfall rates of three to four inches per hour were estimated across the Tug Hill by Sunday. Snow totals surpassed 2 feet across Jefferson County by Sunday evening. The rising sun showed the impact of the historic blizzard across the Northtowns Sunday morning. A long lasting lake effect snow event occurred from the late evening hours of the 16th to the morning hours of the 20th. A deep upper level low settled across the region resulting in a band of heavy lake effect snow across Lake Erie. This band of snow first impacted the Buffalo south towns then became more oriented across the city of Buffalo through the day on the 17th.  Lake effect began to the east of Lake Ontario on the morning of the 17th but didn���t significantly increase until on the night of the 17th and on into the 18th.  Late on the 18th, another storm system brought a resurgence to the lake effect off both lakes before a passing trough began to diminish intensity of the lake snows by the morning of the 20th. Selected accumulations for this long-lasting event included 41.0 inches in North Osceola, 24.3 inches in Richland, 22.2 inches in Eden, 21.3 inches in Hamburg, 14.4 inches in Jefferson, 13.1 inches in Warsaw, 12.4 inches in Silver Creek, and 8.7 inches in West Valley.

Selected accumulations for this long-lasting event included 41.0 inches in North Osceola, 24.3 inches in Richland, 22.2 inches in Eden, 21.3 inches in Hamburg, 14.4 inches in Jefferson, 13.1 inches in Warsaw, 12.4 inches in Silver Creek, and 8.7 inches in West Valley.

A deepening coastal storm brought a glancing blow of widespread wet snow to inland portions of the eastern Lake Ontario region the evening of the 15th through the mid afternoon hours on the 16th. The heaviest snow accumulations were over the higher elevations of the Tug Hill. Otherwise, this system produced a wintry mix with little snowfall over the rest of north central New York and western New York. Selected snow totals included measurements of 9.7 inches and 8.5 inches on portions of the northern Tug Hill.

A strong cold front plowed across the lower Great Lakes. Widespread rain and gusty winds accompanied the front as colder air charged across Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. Rain changed to snow at higher elevations first east of Lake Erie, but it took several more hours for rain to change to snow at higher elevations east of Lake Ontario. The lake effect off Lake Erie increased quickly as a west-southwest wind favored a stronger more persistent band of lake effect snow across southern Erie County and Wyoming County. The arrival of an upper level disturbance and additional moisture further enhanced the lake effect. Snowfall rates peaked over 1 inch per hour and lightning strikes were observed during the height of the evening as snow bands moved southward east of both lakes. Considerable blowing and drifting was observed due to the dry nature of the snow and widespread wind gusts in excess of 30 mph. The residence time of the band as well as upslope enhancement favored the largest accumulations in the entire area over the southern Tug Hill east of Lake Ontario. Meanwhile, to the east of Lake Erie, the shifting nature of the bands as well as marginal temperatures in the lower elevations yielding snowfall totals below warning criteria.||Total snowfall during this event was very elevation dependent. Shoreline locations ultimately saw little if any snow accumulation, while over the higher terrain, 15 inches of snow was observed near West Leyden and 9 inches near Lewis in Lewis County.

A very powerful storm across Ontario (972mb) remained nearly stationary from the evening of December 23rd (Friday) through the morning of December 25th (Sunday). An arctic front associated with this storm moved across Lake Ontario and the St. Lawrence valley during the early afternoon hours of December 23rd but the favored unidirectional SW flow across Lake Ontario didn't establish until Friday evening.||Friday evening through early Sunday morning, a very strong southwest flow of 40 to 50 knots across Lake Ontario led to blizzard conditions across southwest St. Lawrence county through Saturday night and near blizzard conditions at times across northern St. Lawrence county, mainly along/west of Route 11. The lake effect band shifted southeast during Sunday morning, bringing heavy lake effect snow to southeastern St. Lawrence county through Sunday evening.||Some of the higher snowfall totals included 20 to 38 inches in Gouverneur, 20 inches in Morristown, 16 inches n West Fowler, 15 inches in Star Lake and 10 inches in South Colton with 6 to 15 inches across northern St. Lawrence along/west of Route 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions across southwest St. Lawrence county with sustained winds 25-30 mph with frequent gusts in excess of 40 mph causing white-out conditions and considerable drifting of the snow. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches to 2 to 3 feet across the area with the greatest along and west of Route 11. Snow and Travel Emergencies were declared.

An extremely powerful winter storm located in the eastern Great Lakes and Ontario delivered strong southeast winds of 25 to 45 mph in the morning hours that caused some scattered power outages. During mid afternoon, a powerful artic front associated with this winter storm moved across northern NY dropping temperatures from the 40s/L50s into the 20s and falling through the night on very strong west winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts 45-55 mph across the northwest slopes of the Adirondacks as well as the St. Lawrence valley. These winds caused additional power outages but not to the extent of the previous weeks snowstorm. In addition, as those temperature free-fell during the afternoon and evening hours, a widespread 1-3 inches of snow fell with combined with sub-freezing airmass caused a flash freeze on all surfaces creating extremely hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southeast winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph occurred during the morning hours before lessening for a few hours before an arctic cold front brought west winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50-55 mph to the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over eastern Canada dragged a cold front through southeastern NY. A pre-frontal trough ahead of the cold front was able to spark severe thunderstorms that produced large hail. More severe thunderstorms developed along the cold front and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter report of 1 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over eastern Canada dragged a cold front through southeastern NY. A pre-frontal trough ahead of the cold front was able to spark severe thunderstorms that produced large hail. More severe thunderstorms developed along the cold front and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Home weather station recorded a 56 knot gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful west coast storm over the weekend and earlier in the week moved into the Northern Plains during mid-week and largely stalled. However, energy was transferred to the Mid-Atlantic states Thursday (12/15) producing widespread snow, freezing rain/sleet and rain across the Mid-Atlantic into NJ/NYC. A coastal low developed off the Mid-Atlantic during Thursday afternoon and lifted slowly north to south of Long Island on Friday and into the Gulf of Maine by Saturday. ||Snow slowly spread across northern New York during the early morning hours of December 16th (Friday) and continued into Friday night before dissipating as snow showers early Saturday. Air temperatures were marginally cold for snow, thus it was a wet, heavy snow that caused numerous power outages (>20K) in the Adirondacks that witnessed over a foot of snow and mixed with rain at times in the Champlain Valley.||Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 8 inches in the Champlain Valley, 6 to 12 inches in the St. Lawrence Valley, 10 to 16 inches in the Adirondack Valleys with 18 to 24 inches in the Adirondacks of Essex and Clinton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet, heavy snowfall of 5 to 10 inches occurred across eastern Essex. Meanwhile, 12 to 24 inches fell across the Adirondacks in Essex county resulting in 10,000 power outages lasting several days. Some higher snowfall totals include; 24 inches in Keene, Moriah and Newcomb, 20 inches in Schroon Lake and 14 inches in Ray Brook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two beachgoers in the Rockaways drowned due to likely rip currents on June 17th. The rip current risk was low for the NYC beaches, but moderate rip current activity was reported across the Long Island beaches, likely due to the large tidal range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two beachgoers fatally drowned at unguarded areas of Rockaway Beach on Friday June 17th. Law enforcement reported that the first victim, a 16-year-old girl, was pulled out of the water off the intersection of Beach 108th Street and Shore Front Parkway. Simultaneously, police reported first responders got the second victim, a man in his late teens or early 20s, who drowned in the waters off Beach 98th Street ��� ten blocks to the east of the first fatal drowning. Both victims were rushed to St. John���s Episcopal Hospital, where they died, police sources said. The two victims were among five people pulled out of the waters off Rockaway Beach on the same day, the other three distressed victims survived.

However, energy was transferred to the Mid-Atlantic states Thursday (12/15) producing widespread snow, freezing rain/sleet and rain across the Mid-Atlantic into NJ/NYC. A coastal low developed off the Mid-Atlantic during Thursday afternoon and lifted slowly north to south of Long Island on Friday and into the Gulf of Maine by Saturday. ||Snow slowly spread across northern New York during the early morning hours of December 16th (Friday) and continued into Friday night before dissipating as snow showers early Saturday. Air temperatures were marginally cold for snow, thus it was a wet, heavy snow that caused numerous power outages (>20K) in the Adirondacks that witnessed over a foot of snow and mixed with rain at times in the Champlain Valley.||Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 8 inches in the Champlain Valley, 6 to 12 inches in the St. Lawrence Valley, 10 to 16 inches in the Adirondack Valleys with 18 to 24 inches in the Adirondacks of Essex and Clinton counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet, heavy snowfall of 5 to 10 inches occurred across eastern Essex. Meanwhile, 12 to 24 inches fell across the Adirondacks in Essex county resulting in 10,000 power outages lasting several days. Some higher snowfall totals include; 24 inches in Keene, Moriah and Newcomb, 20 inches in Schroon Lake and 14 inches in Ray Brook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough out ahead of a cold front was able to spark severe thunderstorms over an unstable environment in southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Traffic lights out from Glen Cove Road to near Route 107 on Northern Boulevard in Glen Head NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough out ahead of a cold front was able to spark severe thunderstorms over an unstable environment in southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 1 inch hail at Long Island Expressway and Commack Road in Brentwood NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms formed along a sea breeze boundary across Long Island. One of the thunderstorms was able to become severe and caused damaging winds as well as large hail in Central Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on Clunan Drive in Coram.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate frontal systems merged to form a powerful winter storm over the Great Lakes from December 22 to December 24 bringing multiple hazards to the region including heavy rain, snow, strong winds and a very cold air mass in its wake.||Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were observed with some localized amounts of 4 inches. The combination of the rain and snowmelt in some places resulted in some flooding and some creeks to overflow their banks.||Strong pre-frontal and post-frontal winds occurred with this event with widespread wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. Such winds resulted in some downed trees and power lines as well as minor damage to some structures. A few power outages occurred as a result.||Snow showers in the wake of the cold front brought mainly light accumulations. However, a pocket of more moderate snow occurred across the northern and central Taconics with snowfall amounts in the 4 to 8 inch range.||Much colder air built into the region in the wake of the front which, combined with the strong winds, led to wind chill values as low as 15 to 25 degrees below zero in some areas. A few warming centers opened up across the region as a result of the cold.||Key impacts: minor flooding, tree damage, power outages.

A high-impact, long-duration winter storm affected the region from December 15-17, 2022. This was an elevation-dependent storm with heavy snowfall for the higher elevations and a mix of rain and snow in the valleys. Snowfall totals of 12-24 inches were common across areas above 1000 feet with mainly 6 inches or less below. A brief period of sleet and freezing rain occurred across portions of the Hudson Valley during the evening hours on December 15 before precipitation changed over to plain rain. Rain then changed over to snow during the daytime hours on December 16.||This was a heavy, wet snow and the weight of the snow caused downed trees and power lines resulting in scattered power outages. Hamilton County was hit hardest with power outages with over 30 percent of customers tracked without power at some point during the event. A few vehicle accidents also occurred as a result of the slippery road conditions. Delays and cancellations occurred at the Albany International Airport. A few snow emergencies and snow removal parking bans were in place across the region.||Key Impacts: Travel Delays, Tree Damage, Power Outages, Vehicle Accidents.

Following the passing of a front, cold temperatures and flow off of Lake Ontario supported lake effect snow showers over N. Oneida County. The highest totals along the northern portion of the county border where nearly a foot of snow was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 4 and 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lake effect snow band dropped south in Oneida County which was enhanced by a 500 mb vorticity maximum. This band dropped 1 to 2 feet of snow along and north of the NYS Thruway. While lesser amounts were observed south of the NYS Thruway, multiple reports in the central part of Oneida County were in the 6 to 12 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 6 and 24 inches.

Such winds resulted in some downed trees and power lines as well as minor damage to some structures. A few power outages occurred as a result.||Snow showers in the wake of the cold front brought mainly light accumulations. However, a pocket of more moderate snow occurred across the northern and central Taconics with snowfall amounts in the 4 to 8 inch range.||Much colder air built into the region in the wake of the front which, combined with the strong winds, led to wind chill values as low as 15 to 25 degrees below zero in some areas. A few warming centers opened up across the region as a result of the cold.||Key impacts: minor flooding, tree damage, power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was observed at the intersection of Clifton Park Road and Rankin Road in the Town of Niskayuna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lake effect snow band dropped south in Oneida County which was enhanced by a 500 mb vorticity maximum. This band dropped 1 to 2 feet of snow along and north of the NYS Thruway. While lesser amounts were observed south of the NYS Thruway, multiple reports in the central part of Oneida County were in the 6 to 12 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow accumulated between 4 and 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm moved through Central New York on the 15th and 16th. Precipitation started out as freezing mix for some areas and snow for others. Any mix became mostly snow during the day of the 15th. Travel conditions became hazardous, especially during the evening commute, as snow accumulated on roadways. Some locations received a break from the snow overnight before it picked back up on the 16th due to a storm moving north along the Atlantic coast. Snow came to an end on the 17th. The highest snowfall accumulations were mainly along and east of I-81.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high-impact, long-duration winter storm affected the region from December 15-17, 2022. This was an elevation-dependent storm with heavy snowfall for the higher elevations and a mix of rain and snow in the valleys. Snowfall totals of 12-24 inches were common across areas above 1000 feet with mainly 6 inches or less below. A brief period of sleet and freezing rain occurred across portions of the Hudson Valley during the evening hours on December 15 before precipitation changed over to plain rain. Rain then changed over to snow during the daytime hours on December 16.||This was a heavy, wet snow and the weight of the snow caused downed trees and power lines resulting in scattered power outages. Hamilton County was hit hardest with power outages with over 30 percent of customers tracked without power at some point during the event. A few vehicle accidents also occurred as a result of the slippery road conditions. Delays and cancellations occurred at the Albany International Airport. A few snow emergencies and snow removal parking bans were in place across the region.||Key Impacts: Travel Delays, Tree Damage, Power Outages, Vehicle Accidents.

Two separate frontal systems merged to form a powerful winter storm over the Great Lakes from December 22 to December 24 bringing multiple hazards to the region including heavy rain, snow, strong winds and a very cold air mass in its wake.||Widespread rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were observed with some localized amounts of 4 inches. The combination of the rain and snowmelt in some places resulted in some flooding and some creeks to overflow their banks.||Strong pre-frontal and post-frontal winds occurred with this event with widespread wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph. Such winds resulted in some downed trees and power lines as well as minor damage to some structures. A few power outages occurred as a result.||Snow showers in the wake of the cold front brought mainly light accumulations. However, a pocket of more moderate snow occurred across the northern and central Taconics with snowfall amounts in the 4 to 8 inch range.||Much colder air built into the region in the wake of the front which, combined with the strong winds, led to wind chill values as low as 15 to 25 degrees below zero in some areas. A few warming centers opened up across the region as a result of the cold.||Key impacts: minor flooding, tree damage, power outages.

Deep low pressure and the passage of a strong cold front brought strong to damaging winds to portions of Western NY and North Central NY from late evening on December 2nd through early afternoon on December 3rd. Though wind gusts over much of the area peaked at less than warning criteria, there was numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines due in large part to recent rainfall and wet ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of trees downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread reports of trees and wires downed.
